meaning: suffix pronoun of the 1st person, plural; identified with the Gurage directional element -na 'toward'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: for more details, see Brockelmann 1908: 309; Barth 1913:59-62. ETHIOPIC: for more details, see Leslau 1951:58.

meaning: object suffix pronoun of the 1st person, singular;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: for more details, see Brockelmann 1908: 307; Barth 1913:35-43.

meaning: with suffix pronouns serves to emphasize the pronominal element, rendered by 'behold!, as for'. The element na is not used with the 2nd person. The other forms are: naya 'as for me, there I am!, here I am!, behold!'; nayo 'as for him'; nayā 'as for her, behold she!'; nayana 'as for us'; nayomu 'as for them (masc.), behold they'; nayon 'as for them' (fem.). It is also used with -wā, -hu in the forms nawā, nāhu 'behold!'; and with the preposition ?aska 'until' in the form ?aska-na, as in ?amsab ?aska-na ?ansasā 'both man and beast', lit. 'from man to beast'; ?aska-na haba 'until'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps in ?in, hn 'behold!' Ar. ?inna, Soq. ?ine, Heb. hinne, Aram. hnh, Ug. hn, Akk. anna, annu 'yes, certainly'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. -n 'as for', Te. -n 'also', Tna. ?anniho 'behold!'. The element n is also used in the copula of several Ethiopian languages, e.g. Amh. nāw 'he is'. See also nawā. For G. -na, see Dillmann 1907:376. See also ?n-II.

meaning: 'too, also, even, even so, the very, likewise, again, as for, for one's part' (e.g. nagusa-ni 'the king for his part'); ?amma-ni 'though, even though'; (wa)la?amma-ni 'but if'; ?amma-ni...wa?amma-ni 'if...or if, be it that...or that'; -ni wa--ni 'as...so, as well...as, as on the one hand...so on the other, both...and'; in a negative sentence it means 'neither...nor...'; -ni wa--ni 'as so, both...and'; it is also combined with the interrogative particles mant, ?ay, mā?ze, ?ayte for the expression of an indefinite meaning; e.g. wa- ?i-manta-ni 'nothing whatsoever', wa-?i-?ayte-ni 'not anywhere'. Note that in the quoted examples the interrogative element is used in the negative.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. -n 'and', n...n 'either...or', Te. -ni 'then', Amh. -anna 'and', Gur. -ni in zani 'the one there'. For more details on Geez, see Dillmann 1907:411-2, 525, 554.

meaning: particle for the expression of direction 'away to', or of time, as in ?ahatta-ne 'in one, in one place, at the same time, together', wasta ?ahatta-ne 'to one place', ?aska-ne 'until'. See also Dillmann 1907:376.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Praetorius 1879:197 suggests the derivation of the Amharic element -n of the direct complement from the particle -ne. The element -ne is perhaps to be analyzed as -n-e and the ending -e is to be compared with -e of -he in G. Amh. k?alla-he 'everywhere', perhaps also with Gurage -e in et-e, and G. Gur. ?ayi-e 'where?'. Geez -ne could also be

meaning: interrogative particle; e.g. ?anta-nu zaya-massa? 'art thou he who ought to come?'; it is combined with -bo, as in bo-nu ?abuhu 'will he give him a stone?'; or in bo-nu za, in bo-nu za-b?aka 'hast thou something?'. is also used to reinforce ?amma, la?amma 'whether, if', e.g. ?amma ?ammassa-nu ?elyās 'whether Elyas will come'; in a negative ques-

'praise, extol, honor, glorify, (Gr.221) recognize as being good, give a favorable opinion, (T.MA) be pleasant' (rather, 'recognize as being pleasant'); ?an?ada, causative; also 'honor'; tan?da, pass. of n?da; na?dī (K*), act. part. of n?da; na?ud 'praised, glorified, (Gr.221) one of good reputation, famous'; na?dat 'praise' (n); na?dān (T.M), na?odannā (T) 'praise' (n); man?ad (K*) 'who praises'; mastan?dān, pl. (Lt) 'who consider one another marvelous';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAr. perhaps n?d 'luxuriant' (which comes close to the meaning of G. 'extol, recognize as being good'), Akk. nādu, na?ādu 'praise'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nā?adā 'praise', Amh. nadā, Gur. nadā 'be arrogant < be praised > become arrogant', passed into Cushitic: Sid. nāda 'praise, glorify'.

phonetics: n?k ?H, (T) n?k ?H 'lead, tin, alloy, plumb line, (T) mixture, blend, stain, spot'; (?abna n?k 'plummet of tin'); na?aka (K*) 'mix various metals';
meaning: Dillmann 665 lists n?k II 'lead' and n?k III 'stain, spot'. However, n?k III is rendered q?alqal 'mixed' in a S?was?w quoted by Dillmann ib. and in T. In view of the fact that n?k II also means 'mixture, blend', it would seem that the meaning of n?k III given by Dillmann belongs to n?k II.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Akk. anāku 'lead, tin' > Heb. ?anāk 'tin, lead, plummet' > Syr. ?anākā (also Md. anka) > Ar. ?anuk, G. n?k (see Fraenkel 153, Salonen 1952: 6). The noun is considered to be of non-Semitic origin: Armenian anag, Sanskrit nāga.

meaning: 'groan, sigh, pant, worry, be displeased, be under stress'; n?k?, (T) n?k? 'a groan, groaning, a sigh, panting, lamentation'; n?uk (Gr.221) 'groaning, who groans';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. a-nakā 'pant'. See also nahka, nahqa.

meaning: 'be small, be little, be young, (Gr.220) be diminished, suffer penury'; (zayan?as 'small, minor, lesser, junior, the younger, who is small'); ?an?as? 'diminish, lessen, make less, make small, reduce, consider inferior'; tan?as? 'be considered inferior'; tan?as? (K*) 'belittle one another, consider small'; ?astan?as?, ?astannas? 'deem little, belittle, consider small, render humble'; n?us 'small, little, lesser, younger, minor, low (voice)'; (n?us? or n?us?yāt?yān; see krastiyān) (rad? n?us 'disciple', lit. 'young helper'); n?as? (fem.) 'small, minor, young, young girl'; n?as? 'smallness, littleness, youth, misery'; n?s? 'childhood, infancy, youth, smallness, status of a minor'; n?as?onnā 'childhood, infancy'; n?as?āt? 'youth, status of youth'; ?an?as?ī, participle of ?an?as?; man?as? 'youth, little, small, a little, a little piece, a small amount, a few';
'be bad, be hostile'; perhaps a denominative of n?k 'lead, stain, spot', see below.
babbanastit, babbanastit nastit 'little by little, gradually, slowly';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: It is difficult to determine the root, that is to say, whether it is nys as in North Ethiopic, or n's as in South Ethiopic. If the root is n's, the South Ethiopic ns would then be a metathesis of n's. If the original root is the South ns, it is to be compared with Semitic: Soq. 'enes 'be small', Heb. ns ('ni'al) 'be sickly, decrease', Akk. enesu 'be weak', Ug. ans (Caquot 267). Rabin 1978:401 connects it with Ar. an's 'kind'. Koehler 70 derives Heb. 'issa 'woman' from ns/s (for the other Semitic languages, see ib.). The root is not to be compared with Sh nesan 'small' analyzed by Bittner 1916:31 as nes-an. ETHIOPIC: Tna. na'a'a 'be small, be little', Te. na'a-sa, Amh. annasa, Har. anasa, Arg. hannasa, Gur. anasa. Note that Rundgren 1955:164, n. 5 analyzes Amh. annasa as coming from a-n-asa, that is, the causative from an original n's.
see ny'.

phonetics: sg. fem. na'i (na'i); pl. masc. na'u, fem. na'a;
meaning: SEMITIC: Heb. na'a (•) 'now, pray thee!' (Barth 1893:18), Aram. n', Syr. ni.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. na'a 'come!'. Te. na'a, Amh.Gur. na. This element is in all the Ethiopian languages a suppletive imperative of the verb 'to come'. See also na'awa II.

phonetics: na'awalt (pl. na'awalt), [T na'awalt] sox (KG) nu'alt sox 'widow whom the late husband's brother marries by levirate';
meaning: ETHIOPIC: Te. na'al 'husband's sister in relation to the wife', Tna. nawalt. Dillmann 676 considers, with reservations, a connection with Heb.Ar. na'al 'shoe', since in the biblical passages quoted by Dillmann the ceremony of levirate seems to be connected with loosening the shoes. The root is perhaps of Cushitic origin if one identifies it with Be. me'ali, m'ali 'brother-in-law, sister-in-law'.

phonetics: na'awa I (yan'aw) sox, na'awa sox (Lt) 'hunt, lay snares, ensnare, catch birds, set an ambush, lie in wait';
meaning: tan'awa, passive; na'awi (pl. na'awayan, na'awt, [Lt] na'awayat) 'hunter, bird catcher'; na'awit (Lt) 'hunting dog'; na'we, na'awe, (L) na'awe 'hunting'; na'aw (K*) 'hunted'; na'awat 'hunting'; man'aw 'place of hunting';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. na'a 'hunt', Amh. man'aw 'place of hunting' (from Geez), Tna. na'awa 'move here and there' comes close in meaning to Semitic nw: Heb. na'a 'move' (see also Dillmann 676); probably passed into Cushitic: Bil. nawa 'prey'.

phonetics: na'awa II sox (K*) 'call, challenge someone by saying na'a na'a 'come!, come!';
nob sox (T) 'pure gold, plated with pure gold';
meaning: from Coptic noub 'gold'; also Amh. nob 'pure gold'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naba'a 'gush forth, flow', Heb. naba' 'flow', Aram.-Syr. naba, Md. nba, Akk. nambau 'spring' (from nb, nb'). Palache 46 interprets Heb. nb (bif'il) 'speak' as a metaphor of nb 'flow' (for several examples of 'flow' > 'speak', see ib.) contra-ry to Barth 1893:16 who suggests a root nb 'speak' different from nb 'flow'. ETHIOPIC: Te. naba 'shed tears, weep', Tna. naba; Te. anba 'tear', Tna. naba'at, Amh.Gur. Ar. amba, Gaf. amba, Har. abi.

phonetics: nababa (yanbab) sox 'talk, speak, proclaim, pronounce, tell, declare, murmur, utter a sound, resound, be talkative, be endowed with speech, be eloquent';
meaning: unbaba, causative; also 'utter a sound, read, recite, proclaim, chant, study, meditate'; tanbaba 'be spoken, be read, be recited, re-sound'; tanababa 'speak, speak with, speak with another, tell'; astanababa (K*.Gr.219), causative of tanababa 'also 'defend, patronize'; nababi (pl.masc. nababayan) 'one who is a speaker, talkative, eloquent, loquacious, possesses the faculties of speaking and reasoning, rational, reasonable'; (ansasa nababi [Lt] 'rational animal', i.e. 'man'); nabub 'read, recited'; nabib 'word, speech'; ('aqqabe nabib 'orator, advocate'); nabab, nabab 'speech, discourse, utterance of speaking, (Gr.219) diction, pronunciation, last stage of learning the syllabaries, ulties of speaking and reasoning, eloq- (word nabab 'the third stage of learning the syllabary'); Amharism; (qadam nabab 'preface'); nabbat 'reading (n), lection, discourse'; 'anbabi 'reader, lector, deacon who reads the Scripture during service'; 'anbabo, inf. of 'anbaba; also 'speech'; 'anbabot 'diction'; manbab 'place of reading, reading book-ing, scriptural readings, lesson, passage from the Scriptures, lectionary';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nabba (nbb) 'bleat in sexual excitement (billy goat)'; related to Heb. nab (nwb) 'speak' to the root nb' (see nabiyy). ETHIOPIC: Te. naba 'read', Tna. nababa 'resound', nababa 'read', Amh. Gur. a-nababa 'read'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. nabab, Kham. nibeb.
SEMITIC: Ar. nabaha 'bark', Sh. nbh, Heb. Aram.-Syr. nabah, Md. nba, Akk. nabahu. ETHIOPIC: Te. nabha, Tna. nabhe, Amh. anba 'lowing of bovines'.

Can be identified either with Ugar. nbl-t 'flame', Akk. nablu (so also Fronzaroli 1972, v, p. 626, 636), or as a shortened form of nabalbal going back to un-balbala (see nabalbal, and balbala II). Hommel 105, n. 4 derives nabalbal as well as nabal from a root nbl.

phonetics: nabala sox (K*) 'seethe, boil (hot spring), shout, make noise (falling water)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. nabbaba 'seethe', Tna. nabala 'reprimand angrily'.

meaning: transcribes Heb. nebel 'stringed instrument'. For Gk. nablas coming from Heb. nebel, see Masson 68ff. See also nablis.
transcribes Gk. nebel, from Heb. nebel 'large jar'; also Aram. nbl.

phonetics: nablis, nables, nables sox sox 'musical instrument, small bell, harp';
meaning: 'anablasa (T.M) 'ring a bell' (denominative); from Gk. nabla, nablas, from Heb. nebel; also Amh. nablis 'small bell'. Tna. nablis 'one with a loud voice'. See also nabl, above, and nables, below.
perhaps so called because of the sound's being similar to that of nables 'musical instrument', see nablis, above.
perhaps related to Amh. labbaqa 'hit' with alter-nance of liquids.

phonetics: nabara (yanbar) sox 'sit down, be situated, remain, stay, continue, last, endure, abide, persist, live, reside, inhabit, dwell, be';
meaning: 'anbara, causative; also 'place, put, set, set up, seat, establish, lay, lay down, compose, depos-it, settle';

origin_explanation: 'raise, elevate', Ar. minbar meaning 'Erhbung > Sitz, Stuhl', and he states that G. nabara 'sit' is a denominative. Noeldeke 1910:49 (following Schwally, ZDMG 52[1898].146) considers Ar. minbar a Geez loanword. For the role of the minbar in the religious service, see C. H. Becker, "Die Kultus des alten Islam", in Noeldeke 1906:331-51. ETHIOPIC: Tna. Gur. nābära 'sit, stay, dwell, be'. Te. nābra, Amh. nābbärā 'he was', Har. nābära. On the root nbr as an auxiliary in various Ethiopian languages, see Cohen 1924:128, and passim. On the root nbr, see also Rabin, FRCS 1 (1970).288-91. For G. manbar, manbār, cp. Tna. mānbar, Te. mānbar (< mānbar), Amh.Gaf.Gur. wāmbār (from mānbar < mānbar); see Cohen 1931:389-391). Arg. əmbār.

meaning: 'constitute, establish, set, place, put in place, set in good order, put one thing on top of another'
act. part. of nabara; also 'inhabitant, abiding, enduring, steadfast, stable, durable, long-lasting, servant, home-born slave, garrison'
'who sits down, sitting, seated, who stays, who dwells, placed, set, laid down, lying, situated, solid, established, abiding, enduring, residing, resident, inhabited'
'chief ecclesiastic head of particular big churches such as Aksum, and at present also of Addis Ababa', Lit. '[consecrated] by the hands being placed on the head'
'lasting, enduring, (D) home-born slave', (in grammar) 'a noun not related to a verbal stem'
'position, disposition, conduct, placing, arrangement, institution, constitution, (Lt) dwelling'
'sitting down (n), posture, position, site, dwelling, domicile, habitation, abode, state, situation, affairs, establishment, session, stability, existence, life, livelihood, manner of life'

part. of 'astanābära; manbar (pl. manābərt) 'seat, chair, base, socle, residence, dwelling, high place, pulpit, throne, see (of bishop), altar on which the ark (tābot) rests, session, office, function, state, position', (manbara tābot [Lt] 'wooden box in which the ark (tābot) is kept', lit. 'the throne of the tābot'); (manbara fəth 'tribunal'); manbār 'seat, throne, abode, dwelling, position, base, place where something is put, place of residence'; manbart 'state, condition, mode of life, way of living, subsistence, sustenance'; manbārt (Jer.52:19) 'cups'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Praetorius, ZDMG 61 (1907). 618 derives Ar. minbar 'pulpit' and G. manbar from Ar. nabara

meaning: 'heat, fever, kind of disease, sleep' (see also nabašrāw);
origin_explanation: the verb is perhaps a denominative from n which comes from a non-attested 'anbaršaw related to baraša (see above).
from Gk. niptēr, acc. niptēra 'basin'.

meaning: 'prophet, one who predicts' (hassawi [hassawe] nabiyy 'false prophet') nabiyāt, nabayāt, nobəyāt (pl. of nabiyy)'prophets'; (mašafa nabiyāt 'prophetic books, chronicles, annals'); nabiyyət, nabit 'prophetess'; nobəyy (K*) 'which is prophesied'; nabiyyāwi 'prophetic'; tanabbaya 'act as a prophet, prophesy, p (a denominative); 'anabbaya, causative of tanabbaya; also 'establish as a prophet'; tanabbāyi 'who predicts, who prophesies, who acts as a prophet'; tanbəy (L.K*) 'predicted, prophesied'; tanabbayo 'prophecy'; tanbit 'prophecy, oracle, prediction, book of prophecy'; tanabbayot 'prophecy, office of prophet'.
origin_explanation: from Ar. nabīy 'prophet' (so Jeffery, Language 1959].98) rather than from Heb.-Aram. nābī (?), as suggested by Noeldeke 1910:34. The root is represented in other Semitic languages: SAr. t-nb 'promise', Syr. nəbīyā 'prophet', Akk. nabū 'prophet'. For Ar. nabīy < Aram. nabiyyā < nābī (?), see A. Fischer 1953:131. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ə)nābba 'prophesy', Tna. nābīy 'prophet', tānābyā 'prophesy', Amh. (tā 'prophesy', nābī 'prophet'.
probably from Amh. nābbäzä 'rob'.

meaning: 'drive, drive away, lead off, urge on, spur, impel, cause to be inflamed, be on fire, burn up';
'drive away (to take for oneself), rob, commit robbery'
'means of driving (whip, prod), fee paid a herdsman';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. nadaha 'make walk (camels)'; so also Dillmann 681. Buhl 486 compares Heb. nd 'remove from'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nādäw 'send, expel', Tna. nādäw 'push', Amh. nāddäw 'drive', Har. nadäw-a 'guide' (Leslau 1937-549), (tā'nadäw-a 'resign oneself', lit. 'be pushed, be guided'. For G. məndäw 'fee', cp. Amh. mənda, bənda; for G. məndäwāwi, cp. Amh. məndäñña. See also nadha.
could it be an interpretation of the biblical proper name nādāb?

meaning: 'burn (fire, anger), blaze, flame, become aflame, be inflamed, be on fire, burn up'; (zəyənaddəd 'burning'); 'andada 'burn (tr.), light a fire, kindle a fire, set afire, ignite, inflame'; tanādada 'be inflamed'; nadādī 'burning, blazing (fire, anger)'; nədud 'burning, flaming, burned, set on fire'; nədäd 'fever'; nadd 'flame, fever'; naddāwī (M) 'burning, blazing'; nədādāt, verbal noun of nadda; also 'flame, fever'; 'andādī 'who lights a fire'; məndad, in beta məndad (Lt) 'kitchen'; məndad (pl. məndādət) 'material that burns'; məndād 'fireplace, furnace, oven, (Lt) kitchen'; mastanādād (Lt) 'who stirs, who helps to light a fire'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps related to Ar. nadawa 'put over a fire (meat)', nadiyy 'boiled dish'. Heb. nəd, nad of Is.17:11 is translated 'burn up' and connected with G. ndd by Driver, JTS 34 (1933). 380, and Eitan, HUCA 12-13 (1938).65. ETHIOPIC: Te. nādā 'burn', Tna. nāddādā, Amh. nāddādā, Arg. nāddādā, Har. nādādā; passed into Cushitic: Kam. nadəggo. For məndād 'fireplace', cp.

meaning: 'throw, hurl (spear, arrow), shoot (with bow and arrow), sting (insect), strike, transfix, pierce, pierce through, (K*T) draw a circle with a compass for laying a foundation (Amharism), card cotton';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nadāfa 'card cotton by striking the string of the bell with a mallet, send down (rain: cloud), strike, pluck (a lute)'. Ar. (Hadr) 'hit', Heb. nādap 'drive away', Ug. ndp 'expel' (for which see Caquot 522). For the Hebrew root, see D. Leibel, Leshonenu 29 (1964).3-5; H. Rabin, ib. p. 208. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nādāfā 'sting'. Te. ta-nāddafa 'be infected by the māndāf-disease'. Lit. 'be stung'. Amh. nāddāfā 'sting, card cotton, sketch an outline', na-dāfī 'a disease' (lit. 'stung'). Arg. nāddāfā 'sting'. Har. nādāfā, Gur. nādāfā. Note also Tna. māndāfā 'be struck down by the māndāf-disease' (denomi-native from māndāf). The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. nadāf 'throw'.

meaning: 'drive, push, shove, strike, kick, throw down';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. nadaha (4th form) 'push (a boat on the bank of a river)', Heb. nādah 'wield (an axe)', (Hif'il) 'drive out', Aram. nādah 'push'; related to Ar. najaha 'push, thrust', and also to dhh (Heb.

meaning: 'be poor, be destitute, be reduced to poverty, be deficient (in)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. nādya 'be poor'. Tna. nādāyā, Amh. nādāyā, Gur. nādāyā, Har. nādāyā.

phonetics: (dhh, [nif'al] 'be thrust down'), and dhy (Heb. dāhā 'push', Ar. dahā 'drive', Aram. Syr. dāhā)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. nādha 'tread', Tna. nādha 'push'; related to nad'a (see above).
SEMITIC: Ar. nādala 'pull out, take away'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nādla 'pierce', Tna. nādāla, Amh. nāddāla, Arg. nāddāla, Har. nāddāla, Gur. nāddāla 'go out'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. nādal 'make a hole'.

meaning: 'build up, build up construct, make a wall, wall up';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the comparison with Aram. nidbāk 'course of stones', Ar. midmāk (from Aramaic, according to Fraenkel 12) suggested by Praetorius 1890:42 is doubtful. For Aram. nidbāk, cp. Akk. natbāku (von Soden 766). ETHIOPIC: Te. nādqa 'build', Tna. nādāqa, Amh. nādāqa; passed into Cushitic: Sa. nadeq.
from Ar. nazara 'look at', possibly referring to a disease caused by the evil eye.

meaning: 'be poor, be destitute, be reduced to poverty, be deficient (in)';
'make poor, reduce to poverty, im-poverish, (Lt) reduce to nothing';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. nādya 'be poor', Tna. nādāyā, Amh. nādāyā 'poor' (from Geez).

meaning: 'be dried up, dry up, cease to flow, be exhaus-ted, (T) 'ansafa 'dry up (tr), exhaust, drain out',
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. nadaba 'dry up, be absorbed by the ground'. The connection with Akk. nasābu 'einsaugen' (von Soden), 'suck' (CAD) suggested by von Soden 755 is doubtful, the basic idea being 'be dry' rather than 'suck in'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāsfa 'become dry', Tna. nāsāfā, Amh. nāttāfā 'dry up, stop giving milk (cow)'. Gur. (Endegeñ) no'offā (for :d:f, see Leslau 1979:14.2).

meaning: 'strike, break, smite, crush, tear to pieces, dash to pieces, destroy, shatter, knock down, overthrow, throw down, throw to the ground, cast down, trample, fall over heels';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. nataha 'push, thrust (with the horns)', Heb. sānaḥ in Jos.15:18, Jud. 6:21 is rendered 'pierced through' by Gottstein, VT 6 (1956).99, and compared with G. ndh. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāshe 'push, turn over'.

meaning: (Y) nasara 'rip up, tear off, rend, separate, split';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. n-t-sr 'be torn'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāra 'tear asunder'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. načar.
SEMITIC: Ar. nadw (ndw) 'dwindle, abate, decline, disappear (water absorbed by the soil)'; perhaps SAr. ndw 'destroy' (W. Müller).
corruption of Ar. naf'a'a rendered by Dillmann 780 (under 'anpänpe) 'pustulas contraxit' (Lifchitz 161, n.3).
probably for naf'a and borrowed from Ar. naf'a'a 'be useful > become of value, become strong'; also in Tna. nāf'e 'be courageous', Te. naf'a 'able'.
wrong transcription of Gk. naphēlēim (from Heb. napīlīm 'giants', with confusion between the Greek letter l and d (Dillmann 706).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāfāfā 'be a fool', Amh. nāffāfā.

meaning: 'not to find, be avaricious, be stingy';
origin_explanation: from Amh. nāffāgā 'be stingy'; also occurs in Te. nāfga, Tna.Gur. nāfāgā; passed into Cushitic: Bil. nafag 'be avaricious'. Qab. nuf'ga 'avaricious'.
seems to be a misprint for taffuh (see below).

meaning: 'blow, blow upon, breathe on, inflate, sound (an instrument), blow (a horn, trumpet)'; 'anfawa, causative of nafha; also 'cause to swell, bloat (the stomach)'; tanafha, passive of nafha; also 'swell, (Lt) be-come angry, sulk'; nafḥāwi (Lt) 'self-importance'; manfəḥ (K*) 'bellows'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nafaha 'blow (wind)', nafaha 'blow, inflate', Mh. nafəḥ; perhaps SAR. m-nəfəḥ-t 'water dis-tributor', Heb. nāpaḥ 'blow upon', Aram.Syr. napaḥ, Md. npa, nph, Ug. *nph in mphm 'bellows', Akk. napāḥu 'blow upon'. For Ar. nf with 3rd radical t, ġ, ḥ, k, s expressing the meaning 'blow', see Canti-neau, BSL 44 (1947-8), fasc. 2, p. 175. ETHIOPIC: Te. näfḥa 'blow', Tna. näfḥe, Arg. näfḥa-ha, Har. näfḥa, Amh. näfḥa, Gur. nafə; also in Cushitic: Som.Sa. naf (see Dolgopolsky 177).
transcribes Gk. naphéleim, from Heb. nəpīlīm 'giants'. As for KG's 'weak', could it be due to the interpretation of Heb. nəpīlīm 'miscarriage'? See also näfdem.

meaning: 'drizzle, fall in small drops'; nafnəf, nafnəf, nafnəf 'drizzle, shower, moisture, drop, drip'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nafnafa 'drizzle' (Dozy), Ar. (Dat) nafāğ 'drizzle' (n), related to Heb. nāp (nwp) 'sprinkle (see on this root Driver, ZAW 9[1932]. 142). The connection with Soq. nef 'make a sign, a movement', Akk. nāpū suggested by Fronzaroli 1972, VIII, p. 633, and with Ar. nafnaf 'air' (nafnafa 'blow' does not seem to be attested), Heb. nwp 'agitate' suggested by Conti 1978:96 is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Te. näfnəfa 'sprinkle', Tna. näfnəf bälä 'drizzle', Amh. näfännäfä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. nifnif.

meaning: 'eat a lot, rend with the teeth'
origin_explanation: From Amh. näfännäfä 'eat and drink a lot'.

meaning: 'tear off, tear away, rend, divide, divide in two, separate, cleave asunder, split'; nafəqqä 'divide, divide in two, have doubts, doubt, be doubtful, be skeptical, be hesitant, be unbelieving, be a heretic'; 'anfəqa (K*), causative of nafqa; 'anfəqaqa 'cause to be doubtful, lead to heresy'; tanafqa, passive of nafqa; tanafəqqä 'be divided, be divided into factions, be divided against itself, be half-hearted, be a heretic, be hypocritical, be of two minds, be in disagreement, rebut'; nafəqqi, act. part. of nafqaqa; nafəqqi, act. part. of nafqaqa; nafəqqi 'divided in two, divided, separated, split'; nufuq 'one who is doubtful, hesitant, unbelieving, hypocrite, schismatic'; nəfəq 'half, middle, semi-, (Lt) measure of ce-reals'; (nafqa lelit 'midnight'; nafqa diyäqon 'assistant deacon, subdeacon'; nafqa qasis, nafqa kähan 'assistant priest'); nafəqä 'half, middle'; nufəqqe 'division, separation, dissension, doubt, hypocrisy, heresy, skepticism, dissimulation, ambiguity, offense'; nafəqqo (T) 'heresy'; nafəqat 'half, cut piece, section, schism'; nufəqeyät (Lt) 'heresy'; tanafəqqo 'religious dispute, doubt, dissension'; manfəq 'half, faction, part, sect, halfway point'; manfəq ... manfəq 'one part ... (and) another part'; (manfəqa lelit 'midnight'); (manfəqa diyäqon 'subdeacon'); manfəq 'heretic, schismatic, hypocrite, half-hearted'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nafqaqa 'remove oneself, disappear > separate (oneself from others)', Aram. Syr. nǝpaq 'go out', Ug. npq 'go out', Md. npaq; Ar. nafqaqa 'dissimulate, play the hypocrite', munāfiq 'hypocrite' are taken from Geez (Noeldeke 1910:48; Jeffery 272). ETHIOPIC: Amh. näfäqqäqä 'divide', Gur. näfäqqäqä; Tna. nafäqqä 'be a heretic', that is, 'separate oneself from the Orthodox belief', Amh. nafäqqäqä. For nafqa diyä-qon 'assistant deacon', cp. also Tna. nafəqa diqquna.

SEMITIC: perhaps SAR. nfq 'binding, effective', Šh. nfq 'manage to do well'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. näfäqqä 'yearn, long for', Amh. Gur. näfäqqäqä; Arg. nafäqqä- Noeldeke 1910:49 connects it with nfq (see nfq I) and states "das W bezeichnet das geteilte Herz, wie es anderseits im Amharischen vom dringenden Sehnen und Dauern steht".
SEMITIC: SAR. nfq 'sarcophagus'.

meaning: 'bubble, be boiled, boil, (Y) 'anfara 'boil (tr.), (T.Y) scorch, scald'; 'nafara 'be boiled'; tanafara (K*) 'be at odds with one another, rivals'; nafur (K*) 'boiled'; nafr (K*) 'mash (of boiled cereal)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The basic meaning of the root nfr seems to be that of 'jump, fly > boil, bubble'; cp. Ar. nafara 'run away', Ar. (Dat) nafar 'jump', Syr. npr (af-el) 'put to flight', Ug. npr 'flee' (Caquot 128, n.i), npr 'fowl'. Noeldeke 1910:186 compares Syr. npar 'snort' with G. nafara 'boil'. ETHIOPIC: Te. näfra 'jump', Tna. näfära 'fly away', näfärä 'run away', Amh. näfärä 'boil, be boiled', bälä näfärä, Gur. näfärä 'be very hot (from being boiled)'. For G. tanafara, cp. Amh. tänafäfärä. For the connection of the root nfr with the root prr (frr), see Conti 1980:53.

meaning: (nafsat hayəwət, nafsa hayəw 'living creature'); (k*allu za-nafs 'all mortals'); nafsāt (L), nafsāt (T) 'body, genitals, (L) per-son, penis'; nafäs 'wind, air, spirit'; nafäsāt 'climate'; nafsāwi 'spiritual'; nafsāwi 'windy, pertaining to air'; 'anfäso 'refreshment'; manfəs (pl. manfəsāt, manfəsī) 'spirit, breath, spirit of God, wind, a spirit (demon, angel), essence, spiritual body, person'; (za-manfəs 'spiritual'); (manfəs qəddus 'the Holy Spirit'); manfəsāwi 'spiritual, of the spirit, hermit'; mastanfəs (Lt) 'breath'; 'əstanfəs 'breath, breathing, (Lt) inspiration'; 'blow (wind, spirit)'; 'anfasa 'breathe, exhale, make breathe, rest, find rest, revive, refresh, give rest, give soothe'; 'astanäfasa 'make breathe, breathe freely, revive'; nəfs (pl. nəfsāt) 'soul, spirit, breath, a person, life, self' (with suffix pronouns; e.g. nəfsu 'himself')
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nafasa 'breathe', nafsa 'soul, spirit, per-son', Sar. nfs 'soul', Heb. npš (nip'al) 'take a breath', nepeš 'soul, breath, throat', Aram. npš 'soul, person', Syr. napšā 'breath of life, soul', Ug. npš 'soul, person', Ph. npš 'self, person', Akk. napāšu 'breathe', napištu 'life, breath, throat, person'. A metathesis of the root occurs in Heb.Aram. nšp 'blow': Akk. našāpu 'blow away', Soq. nḥof 'soul' (with ḥ = s, š). See D.W. Thomas, "A Study in Hebrew synonyms: verbs signifying 'to breathe'", ZS 10 (1935).311-4; and Fronzaroli 1964, II, pp. 21-3. ETHIOPIC: Te. näfsa 'blow (wind)', tänäfäsa 'breathe', näfəs 'soul', Tna. näfäsä 'blow (wind)', tänäfäsä 'breathe', näfəsi 'soul', näfäs 'wind', Amh. näfäs 'blow (wind)', tänäfäsä 'breathe', näfəs 'soul' (also näbs), näfäs 'wind', Gur. näfäsä 'blow', näfəs 'soul', näfäs 'wind', Arg. näfəs 'soul', Har. näfsi. See also nəsfs, and tanfasa.
see nafasa.

meaning: 'flee, escape, be scattered, vanish (water)'; 'anfasa 'drive away, put to flight, make escape, winnow, (Lt) peel, scatter'; nafäsi 'who flees, who escapes, fugitive'; nufäse (Gr) 'flight'; nafäsii 'the portion that has escaped (dis-aster), fugitive, (Lt) remnant, survivor'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nafasa 'bring out fast, utter (words) fast', SAR. nfs 'march off' (W. Müller, in Höfner
transcribes Heb. naphtālīm 'wrestling'

SEMITIC: Ar. nafata 'sneeze', Soq. nēfoṭ 'blow the nose'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāfäṭa, Te. nāfäṭa, Amh Gur. ṭā- nāfäṭa; Har. anfiṭ 'nasal mucus', Amh. Arg. nafiṭ. For Amh. afanča 'nose', see Leslau 1979:452, under (ṭā)nafäṭa.

from Ar. naft; also Syr. neptā. Akk. naptu, Amh. naft 'rifle' (obsolete), naftānna 'rifleman'. Zimmern 60 considers Akk. naptu to be the origin of the Semitic root nfiṭ (Ar.Aram.) and of Gk. naphtha 'clear combustible, petroleum'. For Ar. naft, see Dozy II 703, and Ayalon, Index, naft.

meaning: 'sift'; tanafaya 'be sifted'; nafayi (K*) 'who sifts'; nafuy 'sifted'; manfe 'sieve'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Perhaps Soq. nofe 'pierce, make a hole', PBH. npy (nif'al) 'sift', denominative from nāpā 'sieve', from Heb. nāpā 'swing', Aram. npā (npy) 'sift', Akk. napū 'sieve' from a root npy (nfy) and compares it with Ar. nafā (nfy) 'sweep away (dust), raise (dust)'; so also Fronzaroli 1972, vii, p.633, and Conti 1978:95. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāfäyä 'sift', Te. Har. nāfa, Amh. nāffa. Tna. mānfiṭ 'sieve, filter'. Te. mānfet, Amh.Har.Gur. wānfiṭ, Arg. wānfiṭā. For m > w in a root with a labial, see Cohen 1931:389-91. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. nafay 'sift'.

meaning: (pl. nagayāt, nagāyāt, nagayāt) 'elephant'; qarna nage 'ivory, elephant's tusks';
origin_explanation: from Sanskrit naga (Hommel 325, 374, and pas- sim); see also nagot.

meaning: 'leguminous plant which has oily black seeds';
origin_explanation: is the Amharic form for nūg (see below).
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Tna. nāg'a 'who grows fast', nagu 'who surpasses'. The Geez ng' (in with ng'') 'go up' develops into 'grow'.

meaning: (yang'a) (K*.DTW) 'cracking sound, crack, shout, be broken, break (int)'; 'ang'a (K*.DTW) 'causative; also dislocate'; 'astanāg'a (T) 'break to bits, incite, (Lt) remove (fat)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. nāga' 'touch, hurt', nega' 'deed of violence', Md. nga 'strike, injure'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāg'e 'break' (intr.), Amh. 'snap, crack, make a snapping or cracking a-nāgg'a 'break', an-nagga 'disjoin, dis- shatter', Gur. (ṭā)nagga 'clash (cattle, obj- ects)'.

meaning: (K*) 'be dry, be destroyed, (Y.M) burn, set fire to';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the basic meaning seems to be 'be be which cp. Soq. ngb 'be dry', Heb. negeb 'the ern part of Palestine' from ngb 'be dry', Aram. nagab 'be dry', Syr. nageb. See also nageb.

meaning: 'south, southern region (p- larly the southern part of Palestine)';
origin_explanation: transcribes Gk. nageb, from Heb. negeb 'see region of Palestine'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. nageb; see nagaba, above.

meaning: (yəngəd) 'go on a journey, travel on business, trade, go on a pilgrimage, go abroad'; nāgada 'travel'; 'angada (K*). caus. of nagada; also 'pro- defer' (lit. 'make something travel for a time'); tanagada 'happen to be a stranger or a g- 'journey here and there, travel on business, travel about, trade, be received a guest';
origin_explanation: astanāgāda (T) 'receive guests'; nagādi 'traveler, trader, merchant, pilgrim'; nagādit 'group of travelers, (Lt) caravan of merchants'; nagid 'journey, commerce, trade, giving h- tality'; nagd 'travel, traveling, trade, business, tra- nagd (pl. nagdān) 'foreigner, stranger, tra- merchant, guest, pilgrim'; (beta nagd 'guesthouse, shop'); nagdat 'wandering, journeying, traveling, journey, voyage, peregrination, departure; nagdənna 'sojourning, traveling in fo- parts'; tanāgādo 'travel, commercial negotiation, trade'; tanāgādot 'pilgrimage, sojourning'; mangad (K*) 'who travels, who trades'; mangad 'road, journey, way, traveling, se- out, trip, pilgrimage'; 'əngədā, 'awangada, see above; SEMITIC: Dillmann (followed by Noeldeke 1910) connects it with Syr. nagad 'flow'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāgda 'go on a pilgrimage', na- 'traveler', Tna. nāgädā 'travel for business, t- nāgādi 'traveler', māgädī 'road'; Amh. nāg- 'travel about, trade in, be a stranger', nāggade 'merchant', māngād 'road', 'əngəda 'guest'; Gur. na- 'travel', nagd 'business', nāggade 'traveler', n- 'stranger, guest'. There is no reason to con- nagada a denominative of mangad 'road' an- identify mangad with G. mangala 'toward' as sug- gested by Praetorius, ZDMG 57 (1903).273.

meaning: (pl. 'angād, 'anāgəd) 'tribe, clan, stock, kindred, progeny, lineage, family';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 695 (followed by Barth 1893) compares it with Heb. neked 'progeny'. For relationship k:g, see Barth, ib. The connection Ar. nagl 'progeny, issue' suggested by Dillmann and Barth 1893:33 is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. nāgäd 'race, tribe'.

meaning: (yəngaf) 'shake off, shake out, knock off, jolt, dispel, brush away, cut off, lay away, throw down, cast, trip, carry away';
origin_explanation: (nagafa lāha 'console oneself', lit. 'shake off mourning'); 'angafa, causative; also 'take away, rescue, drop, make cease, snatch away, make drop, make fall by shaking, shake, drive off'; tanagafa, passive of nagafa; also 'fall off, lose leaves'; tanagāfa 'disengage oneself, free oneself, shake off, shake oneself, bestir oneself'; nagāfi, act. part. of nagafa; naguf, pass. part. of nagafa; nagf, naggāf 'what is shaken off, what is cast out, outcast'; nagfai, verbal noun of nagafa; 'angāfi, mangafi, act. part. of 'angafa; SEMITIC: Ar. nagafa 'cut down (a tree)', Ar. (Dat) nagaf 'winnow', Šh. ngef 'throw away', Soq. negof 'disperse', Heb. nāgap 'strike, smite', Aram. nagap. Barth 1893:34 compares Heb.G. ngf with Ar. naga- fa 'hit' (so also Reinisch 1902:38). Noeldeke 1910: 197 compares Ar. nagāfa 'be agitated, be in commo- tion' (with alternance n:w); see also Conti 1980:82. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāgfa 'shake, shed', Tna. nāgāfa; related to gff, gfgf: Te. gāffa (gff) 'take away', Amh. gāffafa 'lay bare, skin', tāngāfāfā 'shake', passed into Cushitic: Bil. nagaf. The root is also related to naqafa, below.

meaning: (yəngəh) (T), (T) nagha 'dawn, grow light'; 'angəha 'do something early in the morning'; nagh 'early morning, dawn, daybreak'; ('afa nagh 'dawn'); (za-nagh 'matin prayers'); naghat 'becoming dawn' (n);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. nigoh 'become dawn', Heb. nāgah, Aram.-Syr. nagah, Md. nga 'to dawn', nihga 'dawn' (with metathesis), Ug. ngh 'shine'. Akk. nigu 'be clear' given in Leslau 1938:256 is not attested. The connection with Akk. nigu 'joy' suggested by Fron- zaroli 1965, iii, p.145 is doubtful. The connection with Ar. nagaha 'entgegentreten (meist feindlich oder doch unfreundlich)' suggested by Noeldeke 1910:196 is unlikely. The root ngh is related to Syr. gahgah.

meaning: 'be uprooted, (T.Y.M) roll, roll up, make into a ball'; nagal (T) 'rolled, rolled up'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nagala 'remove the skin from a slaughtered animal', Soq. ngl 'make go out', Syr. naggel 'remove'. The root ngl, ngl is the origin of Ar. mangal 'scythe, sickle' (that is, 'instrument that removes, uproots'). Heb. maggāl, Syr. maggālā. ETHIOPIC: Amh. näggälä 'be uprooted'; related to näqqälä (see below). It is possible that nagala 'roll up' is to be separated from nagala 'be uprooted' and to be compared with 'an-gallaga (see above).

phonetics: (see grgr, 'angargara. nagarg"ār 'ār see g"rg"r, 'ang"arg"ara. nagārit (Lt) 'trumpet, drum' (see Powne 13-16).
meaning: (see grgr, 'angargara. nagarg"ār 'ār see g"rg"r, 'ang"arg"ara. nagārit (Lt) 'trumpet, drum' (see Powne 13-16).
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. näg"ärä 'speak', Tna. näg"ärä, näggärä; Te.Tna.Amh.Arg.Gur. nägär 'word, matter'; Amh. astänagär is borrowed from G. 'asta-nägär.

meaning: 'be visible, be adorned'; mangal, mangal (K*T) 'that which is visible, adornment'.

'become king, ruler, rule, reign'; 'angasa, causative; also 'enthrone, consecrate (an ark)'; tanagāsa (K*) 'rule together, rule jointly'; 'astanagāsa (K*), causative of tanagāsa; nägus (Gr) nag"s (pl. nagast); originally from nagasi (pl. nagastat) 'king, ruler'; nagasi (pl. nagastat) 'queen'; nagasi (pl. nagast) 'king ruler'; (mashafa nagast qadamāwi 'First Kings', corre-sponding to First Samuel; mashafa nagast dā-g"amāwi 'Second Kings', corresponding to Second Samuel; mashafa nagast sālāšāwi 'Third Kings', corresponding to First Kings; mashafa nagast rābā'āwi 'Fourth Kings', corresponding to Second Kings'); nagasit 'queen'; nagast 'reign, rule, sovereignty, royal dignity, royalty, kingdom, majesty, festivity at the occa-sion of the consecration of the ark'; nags (Lt) 'hymn sung in honor of the saint of the day'; nagast 'royalty'; nag"usāwi 'royal'; mangas 'tribute'; mangast 'kingdom, kingship, government, reign, majesty, realm, royalty, dominion'; mangastāwi (Gr.223) 'royal'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nagasa 'pursue, press, drive vehemently', Sar. ngs; 'impose (tribute)', Heb. Ug. 'exact tribute, drive to work, press'. Ug. ngs 'overseer' (Ullendorff, JSS 7 [1962].340). See Conti Rossini, RSO 10 (1925).481-3. Note that nagasi passed into Ar. 'al-nagāši 'king of Ethiopia' through Sar. ngsy-n (W. Müller 1978:310). ETHIOPIC: Te. nägsa 'become king, rule', näg"asä, Amh. näggäsä, and probably also the other languages; passed into Cushitic: Bil. nagaz, Bil. nogez.
from Gk. ainigmatistai (pl) 'who speak in riddles'.

meaning: 'say, tell, speak, talk, relate, recite, proclaim, declare, report, an-nounce, indicate, inform'; 'angara, causative; tanagara, passive of nagara; (zay'yätnaggar 'inexpressible'); tanagara 'speak to one another, converse, talk together, exchange words, communicate, talk with, speak to, speak with, speak (a lan-guage)'; 'astanagara, caus. of tanagara; nagāri, act. part. of nagara; also 'narrator'; nagārit (Lt) 'trumpet, drum'; nag"gur (K*Gr.223) 'said, told'; nagar (pl. nagarāt, nagārg"ar) 'speech, talk, word, language, saying, pronouncement, discourse, statement, thing, affair, subject, matter, situation'; tanagāri 'who converses, speaker, rational, rea-sonable'; tanagāro (t) 'speech, discussion, conversation'; managar, managar 'who speaks, who reveals, (Lt) crier, herald'; mastanagär 'mediator, who inter-talks with another, (Lt) preacher'; 'astanagär (K*) 'kind of fruit whose seeds are used in making a drink that inebriates a person and makes him loquacious'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Akk. nagāru 'notify', Ug. ngr 'herald' (Caquot 562-3). Eitan 5 compares, with reservations, Heb. rgm 'murmur, slander' (with metathesis) with G. ngr. The root is related to Gr. qr expressing sound; see Leslau 1938:327, Cohen 1947:no.206, Conti 1980:62. ETHIOPIC: Te. näg"ra 'speak', Tna. näg"ärä, näggärä; Te.Tna.Amh.Arg.Gur. nägär 'word, matter'; Amh. astänagär is borrowed from G. 'asta-nägär.

meaning: 'trunk of an elephant'; undoubtedly to be identified with nage 'ele-phant'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. nägot; also in Cushitic: Bil. nug"at, nugat, Sa. nuge.

meaning: 'behold! now that, here, now!'; from nā and hu; Amh. ännaho. See also *na-, above.

'forge, work metal (iron, silver), beat iron or any other metal'; 'anhaba, causative; tanahaba, passive of nahaba; nahābi (pl. nahabt, nahābāyāt) 'worker in metal, smith'; (nahābe bärur 'silversmith', nahābe wärq 'goldsmith', nahābe hassin 'ironsmith, blacksmith'); nahub (K*) 'beaten (metal), worked (metal)'; nahbat 'skill in forging, forging' (n); manhab, manhab, (T) manhab 'furnace, foundry, forge, bellows'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. nahabay 'smith', Amh. nahab 'work iron, beat iron', Noeldeke 1910:56 considers Ar. nahāmiyy 'ironsmith' a Geez loanword while Brockelmann 1908:226 derives G. nahābi from Ar. nahāmiyy by dissimilation. The connection with Ar. Syr. nhm 'roar' suggested by Dillmann 631 is unlikely.

meaning: 'bee'; (nahaba kalb [Lt] 'wasp'); nahaba (K*) 'raise bees, know the nature of bees, gather honey'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nūb 'bee', Sh. nibbet, Ug. nbt, Akk. nubtu. Landberg 1909:1464 compares Heb. nōpef 'honey', so also Koehler 628, Fronzaroli 1969, vi, pp. 297, 303, but not so Buhl 515. Driver, ZAW 50 (1932).143 derives Heb. nōpef from nfy (for Hebrew, see nafaya, above) and translates it 'drip-ping (of honey)'. Yasin 76 compares Ug. nbt with Iraqi Arabic nabāt 'candy'; however, the t of nabāt is a radical of the root which is not true of Ug. nbt. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nahbi 'bee', Te. nahab, Amh.Arg. Gur. näb, Gaf. nab"ä; Amh. nahab 'spread honey', tä-nabä 'be raised (bees)'.
SEMITIC: Ar. nahaga 'take (a route), enter (a road). Heb. nāhag 'drive', Aram. nǝhag. The comparison between Heb. nhg 'drive' and Amh. manga 'herd' suggested by Praetorius 1879:169 is doubtful.

meaning: 'groan, sigh, be sad, be dis-satisfied, be in straits, worry, (Y) feel for, be compassionate, (Gr.212) be tormented, be agitated'; 'anhaka (K), causative; nahak, nähk 'a groaning, sulking, a sigh, (K) grief, sadness'; related to nahqa, below, and nǝ'ka, above. See also nahka II.
'be attentive, scrutinize, reflect' comes close to the meaning 'feel for, be compassion-ate' of nəhka I.
origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. nəham 'compassion', or an interpretation of the Hebrew proper name nāḥūm.

SEMITIC: Ar. nahaqa, naniqa 'bray', Mh. nəhēq, Soq. nahqəq 'snore', Heb. nāḥaq 'cry out' (related to nāḥaq 'groan'), Aram. nāḥaq, Md. nihiqa 'groaning', Ug. nhq-t 'braying of ass', Akk. nāqu 'cry out, groan'; related to Heb. nhq (pi'el) 'moan', Ar. nahi-qa 'pant', Syr. nəḥaq 'groan' (Koehler 598, nhg II), and to naqqa 'sound of animals' (Landberg 1942:2810). ETHIOPIC: Te. nāḥaqa 'bray'. See also nəhka, nə'ka, above, naqawa, below.

meaning: 'flow, go down, leap'; nahara (K*) 'river, that which flows'; mənhār, manhar (K), mənhar, manhar (D) 'irri-gation channel, machine for drawing water, pump, conduit, (T) waterfall, spring, (K) after-birth'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nahr 'river', nahara 'flow', SAr. nhr 'irrigation channel', Heb. nāhār 'river', nāhar 'stream' (v.), Syr. nəḥārā 'river', Ug. nhr, Akk. nāru. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāhārā 'leap'. Amh. narā 'rise up'. The meaning of G. mənhār 'afterbirth' is developed from the meaning 'flow, go down'. See also nəḥra, below, and mənhar, above. G. nahār might be a transcription of Heb. nāhār 'river'.

meaning: 'recover, re-pose, feel relieved, find consolation, find peace, be quiet, be quieted'; 'anhaya, causative; also 'comfort, relieve, ren-der service, give consolation'; tənahaya, tanhaya (T), tanhaya (T.M) tana-haya 'confess one's sins, reveal (the sins), be submissive, receive comfort by confessing, serve, help, relieve'; nəḥāyi (K*), act. part. of nəḥya; nəḥay (K*) 'recreation, rest, tranquility, relief, refreshment, consolation, comfort'.
origin_explanation: nəḥuy 'recovered, quiet, restored, relieved, refreshed'; (nəḥuya [L] 'quietly'); 'anhayo 'consolation, comfort'; manḥay (fem. manḥit), manāḥay, (K*) 'one who gives comfort, who gives consola-tion, consoler'; manḥay (Gr.211) 'place of rest'; tanāḥayo (T) 'comforting'. SEMITIC: the connection with Ar. wahana 'lose strength' suggested by Praetorius 1890:43 is doubt-ful. For the meanings 'recover, receive comfort by confessing, confess sins', the root nḥy is perhaps related to Sar. nḥy 'confess' (Praetorius, ZDMG 66[1912].787, Conti Rossini 1931:185). ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāḥayā, nāḥawā 'feel relieved, feel relaxed', Gur. nayā 'lie down to sleep', Har. nē,a, Amh. añña 'put to sleep' (d'Abbadie 541), ancient Amh. əñ(ñ)allāhu 'I sleep' (Getatchew 1970:71). In Amh. tānna 'sleep', Arg. tenna, the tā- of the tā- stem became agglutinated to *añña (see Praetorius 1879:241). The root seems to be common with Cushitic: Be. nay 'sleep, rest'.

meaning: 'cavity, riverbed, bank of a river, channel';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. nahab 'isthmus' (probably from Geez).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. nadā 'demolish', Gur. nadā (from Amharic).

meaning: 'scratch the face (in mourn-ing), carve'; metathesis of hanfata.
'leguminous plant that has oily black seeds'; (qab'a nəḥig) 'oil made from the nəḥig-plant' is used for cooking, especially during the fast-ing period);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. nəḥig, same, Tna. nəḥig*, nihug, nug; from Cushitic: Sa. nəḥug, Som. nugi. See also above.

SEMITIC: Ar. naḥnu, Mh. nha (also Ar.-Had. Sh. nhan, Heb. naḥnū, 'anaḥnū, Aram. 'anaḥnā, honan, Akk. nīnu. For more details, see E. Mann 1908:299-300, Barth 1913:5-8. ETHIOPIC: Te. həna, Tna. nəḥna, Amh. əñña, əñna, Gaf. əñni, Har. əñña, Gur. ina.

phonetics: nəḥna I, nānəḥna nəḥn (K*), nānəḥna nəḥn (M.Y)
meaning: 'be in abundance, left over, be plenty, boil over'; tanānəḥna (K) 'be in abundance'; 'anānəḥna (K*) 'augment, cause to abound, cause to boil over'; nəḥnuḥ (K*), nəḥnuḥ (T) 'numerous, abundant, which is in excess'; nāḥnāḥe (K*) 'abundance'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. nanna 'be numerous, boil over'.

meaning: 'shake, agitate, break'; tanānəḥna 'be agitated (sea), shake (intr.), toss, rage, roar'; nəḥnuḥ 'shaken, shaken together, agitated, pressed down'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naḥnaḥa 'walk rapidly' (Dillmann 634). For the meaning 'rage, roar', cp. perhaps Ar. naḥ-ha 'clear the throat', Neo-Syr. naḥnaḥ 'pant, puff'. ETHIOPIC: probably also Tna. (tā)nāḥanəḥ 'quarrel', nəḥnaḥ 'violent argument, violent quarrel'.

meaning: name of the twelfth month (corresponding to August 7th-September 5th);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāḥase, Tigre not attested, but probably also nāḥase, Amh. nāḥase, nase, Gur. nase; passed into Cushitic: Qab. nāse'e, Tem. nahāsa.

SEMITIC: Ar. nuḥās 'copper', Heb. nəḥōšet, Aram. Syr. nəḥāšā, Md. nḥāsa, Ph. nḥš-t. For the Aramaic origin of the Ar. nuḥās, see Fraenkel 152, Jeffery 278. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāḥasi, Te.Har. nāḥas, Amh. nāḥas, Gur. nas; passed into Cushitic: Som. nas. Sa.Af. nahas. The Ethiopian nouns are perhaps taken from Arabic.

meaning: 'roof, rooftop, masonry wall, (T) watchtower, balcony, ter-race; fringe of robe'; nahasa (T.Y), nəḥsa (T) 'construct a masonry wall';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. nahsi 'roof', Te. nəḥas 'house made of stone and clay with a roof of pounded clay'. Amh. nas 'wall made of stone and mortar', passed into Cushitic: Bil. nahes 'wall, house with a flat roof', Sa.Af. nāḥsa.
ETHIOPIC: Te. ('a)nḥas (d'Abbadie) 'cavern, cave'.
'for 'brick', cp. nāḥs II 'wall'.
undoubtedly transcription of Heb. nəḥaš 'sorcery'. A more correct G. root could perhaps be nəḥis, nəḥisā, with š.

meaning: 'forget, neglect, overlook'; 'anāḥsaya 'forgive, pardon, overlook, ignore, be indulgent to, forget, neglect, despise, reject, humiliate'; tanāḥsaya (T.M) 'be modest, be humble, hu-miliate oneself, be humiliated'; manāḥsayi (Gr.212) 'one who forgives'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: augmented by h in relation to nsy, nšy of Semitic (Dillmann 633; Stade 43): Ar. nasiya 'forget', Sar. nsy-m (n) 'being forgotten' (Müller 1963:312), Soq. neše, Heb. nāšā (nšy), Aram.-Syr. nāšā, Md. nsa, Ug. nšy (Caquot 243); for Akk. mašū, see Haupt, AJSL 22 (1906), 199, and Brockel-mann 1928:450. On Ar. nasiya, see Rundgren 1968: 165. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'anašayā 'forgive', nəḥsaya 'feeling of compassion, pity, or indulgence'. See also nəḥsw, and nasaya I.

meaning: 'draw blood with a suction cup'; tanəḥwa (T) 'be drawn (blood) with a suction cup'.
'cup for drawing blood'; nāh (T) 'cup for drawing blood'; see also tanha.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)nāḥnaḥa 'clear the throat'.

meaning: (Y) naḥla 'be destroyed, collapse, fall into ruin, give way, tumble down, fall down, become desolate, be lost'; 'anḥala 'destroy, demolish, ruin, overthrow, throw down, knock down, pull down, lay waste'; tanaḥla, passive of 'anḥala; tanaḥala (K*), reciprocal of 'anḥala; nǝḥul 'destroyed, collapsed'; nāḥl 'ruin, destruction'; nāḥl, (Gr.212) nāḥl, nāḥl 'ruin, destruction, collapse'; naḥlai 'ruin, destruction'; 'anḥāli, manḥal, manḥal (K*) 'one who destroys'; manḥāl (K*) 'means of destruction, means of pulling down';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naḥula (with ḥ) 'be emaciated, waste away (Amsalu 1962:34). Heb. naḥalāh 'destruction' in Is.17:11 (Eitan, HUCA 12-13[1937-8].65). Syr. naḥal 'be made thin, be made meager'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāḥalā, nāḥalā 'crumble, fall down'. Te. perhaps a transferred meaning in 'anḥala 'confuse, perplex'.

meaning: 'snore, snort, become enraged'; nāḥr (K*) 'snoring'; manḥar, manḥar 'pair of bellows';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 663 and Praetorius, ZDMG 61 (1907). 615 derive the meanings 'pump' and 'pair of bellows' from the same root, but in fact 'pump' comes from the root nahara (see above) whereas 'pair of bellows' derives from the root nahara. SEMITIC: Ar. nahara 'snort, snore', Mh. naḥr 'nose', Heb. nḥr (pī-el) 'snort', nəḥīrayim 'nostrils', Aram.-Syr. naḥar 'snort', Md. nḥr 'snort', Akk. naḥāru 'snort'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāḥara 'grow angry', Tna. nāḥara 'get angry', nuḥār 'snorting', probably from Ar. naḥāra passed into Cushitic: Bil. naḥar. The root is related to hr, hrr: Ar. ḥarḥara 'snore' (Buhl 498).

meaning: 'injure, harm, damage'; nak 'blight, mildew'; nikā'u (KG 1), nikā'u (KG 2) 'lame'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to nk, nky (see nakaya I). ETHIOPIC: Te. nāk'a 'damage, hurt', Tna. nāk'e 'touch', Amh. Arg. nākk'a 'touch, hurt', Gur. nākk'a 'touch', Gur. nāka. See also nakaya I.

meaning: 'bend, fold'; mankob, mankobayā (K*), mankob, (M), mank'ab (KG), pl. mankobiyāt, mankobayāt 'bending (of the arm or the leg), articulation (of the leg), arm, armpit, groin, thumb, (Lt) handle';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nakaba 'incline', mankib 'the place of the junction of the humerus and the scapula > shoulder' (Lane), side, flank' (Dillmann 668). ETHIOPIC: Te. tankaba 'lean upon', mank'ab 'elbow', Tna. mänk'ub 'shoulder, thumb, big toe'.
perhaps metathesis of nǝḥuk from naḥka 'groan > bray'.

meaning: 'shake, agitate, (Gr.222) hit hard, (T.Y.M) stimulate, excite, trouble'; 'anaknaka 'move';
origin_explanation: naknaka, passive; naknaki (K*), act. part. of naknaka; naknuk 'shaken, agitated'; nakənnaḳe 'shaking, striking together'; SEMITIC: Ar. naknaka 'importune a debtor', 'pester, annoy' related to Soq. neqneg 'shake'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāknaḳa 'shake'. Te. nāknaḳa, nāknāḳa 'shake, agitate'. Te. nāqnāqa, Amh. nāqnāqa, Har. niqniq 'āsa, Gur. nǝqənāqā. Amharic nāqnāq 'shaken, upset'. Tna. nāqnāqā 'be shaken'. The root probably passed into Cushitic: Bil. naknak, Kham. nineq.

meaning: 'separate, make different, be strange, (K*) be admirable'; 'ankara (K*) 'cause to separate, cause to be different, regard as strange, marvel, admire, be bewildered, be amazed, wonder, be surprised, be astonished, astonish, amaze, (Lt) deny'; tanakara, (L) tanakara 'estrange oneself, be a stranger, be different, be rejected, be estranged, be surprised, be admired, be admirable'; tanākara 'be separated from one another, become strangers to one another, be estranged, be repudiated, be alienated from, renounce, reject'; 'astanakara, causative of tanākara; also 'admire, consider strange'; nǝkūr 'uncommon, marvelous, admirable, wonderful, astonishing, glorified'; nakir (fem. nakkār) 'strange, foreign, strange alien, one who is not a relative, other, different, unusual, wonderful, marvelous'; ('amalakta nakir 'strange gods'); (baḥera nakir 'strange land'); nakirāwi 'stranger, foreign, adversary'; nakarā 'wonder, miracle, astonishing thing, admiration'; 'ankāri (K*) 'who admires, marvelous'; 'ankaro 'admiration'; mankari (K*) 'who admires, marvelous'; mankar 'wonder, miracle, marvelous thing, stranger, marvelous, wondrous, wonderful, admirable'; (mankara [L] 'wonderfully, marvelously'); mankərāwi 'marvelous, miraculous, admirable';
origin_explanation: mankarārī (Gr.459) 'who performs miracles'. tanākarot 'rejection'; the basic meaning of this root is 'be separated, different, other, strange, unusual' > 'bewildering, astonishing, admirable, marvelous'. SEMITIC: Ar. nakira 'not know, reject', Sh. nker 'deny', Sar. nkr 'stranger', Heb. nkr (nif'al) 'behave as a stranger', nekār 'foreign land', nokrī 'stranger', Md. nukraia 'strange, different, sublime', Ug. nkr 'stranger', Akk. nakāru 'be a stranger, be a foreigner'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nākra 'reject', mətənkar 'miracle', Tna. 'ankārā 'admire', Amh. nākkārā 'separate', tānakkārā 'be admired', 'anākkārā 'be surprised'. For the roots that express negation, question, and admiration, see P. Jensen, "Ausruf, Frage und Verneinung in den semitischen Sprachen", Zeitschrift für Völkerpsychologie 18 (1888).419-430.

meaning: 'dip, immerse'; tanakra (K*) 'be soaked, be drenched, immerse';
origin_explanation: Probably from Amh. nākkārā 'dip, immerse', also in Tna. nākkārā, Arg. nākkārā, Har. nāxāra, Gur. nākkārā, passed into Cushitic: Al. nākkārā.

meaning: tanākasa (Gr) 'bite one another'; 'astanākasa (Gr), caus. of tanākasa; mankas 'jaw, jawbone'; see nasaka, below.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. nākkātā. If Geez nkt is the original root, Amh. nkt can be explained through assimilation of kt becoming kt.

meaning: 'injure, hurt, damage, harm', (T.Y) = Amh. g"addālā 'be diminished' (should be 'ag"addālā 'diminish', tr.); 'ankaya, caus. of nakaya; also 'harm, dam-
'shepherd, herder, custodian, pastor'; nolot (Gr.22) 'sheepfold'; tanolāwi 'shepherd, custodian, pastor'; nawalwanna (Lt) 'function of shepherd'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Praetorius, ZDMG 43 (1889), 324 derives G. nolāwi from Or. lōn 'herd, flock (with metathesis), with the ending -āwi. For the Or. root, cp. also Sa. lawinā, laynā 'shepherd'. See also nalaw, above, and nolaya, below.
related to nolawa, above.

SEMITIC: Ar. namir, nimr, SAr. nimr, Heb. nāmer, Syr. nemrā, Md. namar, Akk. nimru. On the etymology of namr, see Hommel 295ff. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāmri, nābri, Te. nābri, Amh. nābr. For the dissimulation in nmr:nbr, see Rūžička 91-2.

meaning: 'kind of fruit, bunch of fruit'; nappa (MA) 'produce fruit'
origin_explanation: see also nāpnāp.
see also nāp.

meaning: 'minor rank in the church hierarchy (possibly 'office of an acolyte')'
origin_explanation: G. Salinger suggests, with reservations, a graphic corruption of Gk. akolouthos, G. *'a-h-a-n 'aqolutos corrupted into nipolubos.
wrongly for tapāzayon, from Gk. topazion 'topaz'. Note also G. pāzayon.

meaning: 'be split, burst, rupture, be cleft, be slashed, burst forth, have cracks, become torn, flow, pour out, gush out, up'; yanqa'a, caus. of naqa'a; also 'split, shatter, cleave, tear up, slash, rip up'; naqu 'split, cracked, ruptured, cleft, full of cracks, torn'; naq (pl. anqa'at) 'fountain, spring, (K) crack'; naqa'at 'crack, fissure, crevice, chasm, abyss, hole'; anqa'i, participle of anqa'a; manqa'i, (Lt) manqa', participle of anqa'; manqa'at (pl. manāqa', manāqa'at) 'crack, (T) spring, (D) articulation, heel, ankle, line (of neck), thigh, thighbone, (MA) armpit, counterpart, (K.KG) opponent, (T) members of the body'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naqa'a 'tear', Heb. nāqa' 'split', neq'ā 'cave, hole'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāqa'a 'ooze out, burst asunder', Tna. nāqe'e, Amh. nāqqa 'crack, split, fracture, forth'. For manqa'at 'articulation, members of body', cp. Amh. māllāyaya 'members of the body, articulation', from lāyyā 'separate'. The nouns: man'i 'spring (of water)', Amh.Gur.Arg. manč, mančā are identified with G. mānqa'at. See also qāq.

meaning: 'disjoin, separate, apart, withdraw, (Gr.216.K*) perforate, snatch away, (T.Y.M) scare, terrify, be enraptured'; anqaba (K*), causative; tanqaba, passive of naqaba; naqub (K*) 'pierced, perforated'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naqaba 'bore, pierce' (for its relation with wqb, see Noeldeke 1910:187), SAr. nqb 'cut, excavate', Heb. nāqab 'bore', Syr. naqab 'perforate', Akk. naqābu 'deflower, rape, perforate'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāqba 'seize and tear off', Amh. nāqqābā 'make a hole'. It is possible that the meanings 'scare, terrify, be enraptured' belong to a naqba II.

meaning: 'short skirt, apron, working garment, trousers, loincloth, girdle'
(T) garment that has four hundred threads in width'
origin_explanation: From Ar. naqba 'garment, trousers'

meaning: 'peel, bark, defile, contaminate, blemish, blame, rebut (testimony), stain, spot, (T) separate, (Lt) reject'; anqafa (K*), causative; tanqafa, passive of naqafa; naquf, pass. part. of naqafa; naqaf 'blemish'; naqafat 'contamination, blemish, stain, reproach'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naqafa 'break, split', Soq. neqof 'scrape', Heb. nqp (nif-al) 'be skinned', Syr. nūqāpā 'striking' (n). ETHIOPIC: the original meaning of the Ethiopian root is 'break, break off, tear off', hence 'blemish, rebuke'. Te. nāqfa 'detach, strip off (leaves)', Tna. nāqāfā 'bark, reject, blemish', Amh. nāqqāfā 'blemish, censure, despise, reproach', Gur. nāqāfā 'tear off a branch at the joint'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. naqaf. See also naqafa.

meaning: 'wake up (intr.), keep awake, be watchful, watch over, be alert, be attentive, be vigilant, apply oneself, convalesce, recuperate'; anqāha, causative of naqha; also 'awake, wake up (tr.), rouse, stir up, revive, refresh (memory)'; naqāh 'awaking'; naquh 'awake, diligent, watchful, vigilant, alert, sober'; naqah (L) 'interval between waking up and getting up'; naqāh 'awaking'; naqhat 'vigil, vigilance, awakening, watchfulness, alacrity, application'; naqhāwi 'pertaining to being diligent, awake'; anqāhi (D), anqāhi (K*) 'awakener, who resuscites'; manqāh (Gr.215) 'who awakens'; manqāhi 'who awakens, who resuscites'; manqāh (K*) 'means of being awake'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naqiha 'convalesce' (Dillmann 643). ETHIOPIC: Te. nāqha 'wake up', Tna. nāqhe, Har. nāqaha, Arg. nāqqaha, Amh. nāqqa, Gur. nāqa.
Ethiopic: Tna. nəq'bə 'hatch'.

phonetics: naqala (yənqəl) 'uproot, eradicate, pull up, pull out';
meaning: 'uproot, eradicate, pull up, pull out'; tanaqala, passive; also 'remove oneself'; naqali (Gr.215), act. part. of naqala;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naqala 'remove', Syr. nql 'quarry stone' (?), Syr. nəqal 'clear away, reject', Md. nql 'remove'; Hebr. maqqel 'rod, staff', from *manqel (from nql 'pull out, set out' [see also Tna. naqäla 'rise to set out'], lit. 'instrument serving or used when one sets out'; see Leslau 1958:31. For another opinion, see Rundgren 1961:368. ETHIOPIC: Te. naqla 'take away, tear out', Tna. naqäla 'pull out', Amh. näqqäla, Arg. näqqäla, Har. näqäla, Gur. näqäla. Beguinot 516 identifies Amh. näqqäla with näggäla (see naqala, above).

phonetics: näqil , in həbra näqil (Lt), näqəl (T*) 'speckled color, spotted, multicolored, variegated';
origin_explanation: perhaps from Ar. naqil 'tainted, defective'.
perhaps to be identified with ləm 'pick, pluck, glean' (see laqama, above) with alternance of liquids.
SEMITIC: Heb. naqiq 'cleft'; related to nq', for which see naq'a, above.

phonetics: naq'ara (yənq'ər) 'peck, prick, pierce, make a hole, pluck out, be one-eyed, be blind-ed';
meaning: 'peck, prick, pierce, make a hole, pluck out, be one-eyed, be blind-ed'; 'ənq'ara, causative; also 'to blind, put out an eye'; naq'ər 'pierced (eye), blind'; naq'ar 'one-eyed, cross-eyed, blind'; manq'ar (K*) 'beak, bill' (that is, 'the part of body with which one pecks');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naqara 'pierce, bore', Heb. naqar 'bore', Aram.-Syr. naqar, Md. naqara 'beak, pick', Akk. naqaru 'hew out'. For the relation between nqr and wqr, see Noeldeke 1910:184. For 'beak, bill', cp Pbh. manqora, Syr. manqora, from nqr. ETHIOPIC: Tna. naq'ra 'burst', Te. naq'ra 'pick out, damage', Amh. näqq'ara 'pierce'.
from Ar. niqris 'gout, arthritis' < 'death, death-like condition' (Voller 1897:304); also in Amh.Gur. näqärsa 'scrofula'.

phonetics: naqasa I (T,Y,M,Lt) 'separate, pluck away, extract (a thorn from the foot)';
meaning: 'separate, pluck away, extract (a thorn from the foot)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naqasa 'extract, pull out', Soq. neqos 'pierce (an abscess)', Sh. nqes 'take out (an em-bedded thorn)', Syr. naqas 'cut in pieces'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. näqäsa 'pinch, extract a thorn from the foot', Amh. näqqäsa 'pick, clean, extract a thorn from the foot'. See also naqasa II.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. näqqäsa 'tattoo', Gur. näqäsa. The meaning 'tattoo' could be derived from I 'distinguish', the tattoo being a distinguishing mark, but cp. also Ar. naqasa 'paint', borrowed also by Neo-Syriac naqis, anäqqäsa 'adorn, ornament'.

perhaps from an Arabic dialect in which classical Arabic naqusa, nagisa 'be dirty' is represented as nqs > nqs (so Dillmann 645). The suggestion made by Nyberg 232 to connect it with rks 'be impure' is not convincing.

phonetics: naqsa (MA) naqda 'dry up, be parched, become hard, dwindle away, be ex-hausted, (K*) be split';
meaning: 'dry up, be parched, become hard, dwindle away, be ex-hausted, (K*) be split'; 'anqasa 'make dry, parch, make hard, split'; naqus 'dry, parched, hard, harsh (word)'; naqs (K*) 'dryness, split'; naqsat 'drying up (n), dwindling (n), dryness';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naqasa 'diminish, wane', SAr. h-nqs 'diminish' (Biella 317), Sh nqes 'be a small amount', Md. nqs 'decrease'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. näqäsa 'dry up' (also with metathe-sis, näqäsa 'be hard'), Te. näqasa 'become weak'.
the basic stem naqata is probably reconstructed from the form 'ənqata 'make 'ənqat-soup' taken from Amh. ənqät, denominative ənqäqäta.
from Ar. naqa(t)a; also in Te. nä'at (from an Arabic dialect with q > '). On Aram. Pbh. nä(q)ta, Pbh. 'änäqä 'she-camel', see D. Talshir, in Ben-Hayyim 226ff.

phonetics: naqota, niqota, nekatä, in beta niqotä-hu 'the house of his spices';
origin_explanation: transcribes Gk. nekhōtha, from Heb. (bet) nekot-ō rendered 'house of his precious things'.

phonetics: naq'ata (K*,MA) 'drop, fall drop by drop, mark with spots, be speckled';
meaning: 'drop, fall drop by drop, mark with spots, be speckled'; 'ənq'ata (K*), causative; naq'! 'the four dots marking the end of a sentence';
origin_explanation: from Ar. naqata 'fall in drops, stain, speckle', 'point, dot', also in Tna. näq'ti, Te. näqätat, Amh. näq'ət. In the other Semitic languages, 'point, dot' is expressed by the root nqd: Heb. nəqudda, nuqäda (from which Ar. nuqta is borrowed, accord-ing to Fraenkel 195). W. Müller 1985:272 compares Mh. naged (root nqd) 'scar from burning'.

phonetics: naqatqät, naqutqut, nəqätqätä ; see qətqət II, ('ən)qatqata.
neqetro, neqatro, neqetru, neqeträ 'kind of flower, perfume of a flower';
meaning: 'kind of flower, perfume of a flower';

phonetics: naqawa (yənqu), naqqawa 'emit a sound (referring to any sound of animals or birds), scream, shout';
meaning: 'emit a sound (referring to any sound of animals or birds), scream, shout'; 'ənqawa (K*), causative; tanaqawa (Gr.216) 'crack, squeak, (T) shake, make noise, resound'; 'astanäqawa (Gr.216) 'make resound'; naqawi, act. part. of naqawa; also (Lt) 'herald'; naqaw (Gr.216) 'sound (of animals), (K*) who shouts'; naqaw 'noise, shout, animal sound (bark, yelp, and so on), tone'; naqawa 'shouting, noise'; (nəqwața dərho 'cock's crow');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to naqqa, naq'a 'croak, cackle', Akk. naqagu, näqu 'shout'.

meaning: ETHIOPIC: Tna. nägwa 'shout', Te. näqa (nqw), Gur. näqa; related to nəhəqa (see above); also in Cushitic: Be. a-nkuw 'sound of animals'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. nägwa 'shout', Te. näqa (nqw), Gur. näqa; related to nəhəqa (see above); also in Cushitic: Be. a-nkuw 'sound of animals'.

phonetics: naqza (yənqəz) 'be worm-eaten, be spoiled by worms, rot';
meaning: 'be worm-eaten, be spoiled by worms, rot'; naquz 'worm-eaten, decayed'; naqaz, naqz 'worm which eats grain or wood, moth, being worm-eaten (n), rottenness';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Arg. näqza 'worm that eats wood'; Gur. nəqəz; also in Cushitic: Al. niqisa, Sa. nəqəz.
from Ar. när 'fire', nūr 'light'; see also nwr II, and när-el.

(Juste d'Urbin in Gr.431 renders it 'coupe, chalice'; it would seem that he misread Dill-mann's 672 calx ['chalk, lime'] for calix 'cup, chalice'. The meaning 'coupe, chalice' is therefore not valid); either directly from Ar. nūra or through Amh. nora; also Tna.Gur. nora, Te. norät, Har. nōrät.
combination of när 'fire' (see above) and of the Heb. 'el 'God'; also in Amh. nar'el 'star of the rainy season'.

from Gk. nardos 'nard, spikenard'; also in Amh.Tna. nardos. On the Semitic origin of Gk. nardos, see Masson 56. The Greek form also occurs in Syr. närdin, närdūs, Ar. nardin, nardīn. The other languages have the Semitic form: Heb. nerd, Md. nard, Akk. lardū, Syr. also nard. See on this root Wagner 84-5; for its Sanskrit origin, see Rabin 1968:437.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. narge, Amh. nard, närg 'wooden container for butter, oil, perfume, or spikenard'.

meaning: 'bite', 'be bitten', 'bite one another', 'causative of tanasaka', 'one who bites', 'biting', 'means of biting: jaw, teeth'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Hars. netôk 'bite', Heb. nâšak, Ug. ntk, Akk. našâku, but with metathesis in Ar. 'break', Aram.-Syr. nəqaț 'bite', PbH. nâšak 'break' that comes to mean 'bite' when in contact with something or someone, Md. nkš (considered a Babylonian loanword according to Dietrich 300), nki 'bite'; Hebrew also nešeq 'increase in of a debt, interest' (so Buhl 527 rather than ing it a loanword fom Akk. nikkašu, as sup. Koehler 639); also Ug. ntk 'interest'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nâkâšâ 'bite', Te. nâkšâ, Amh. nâkâšâ, Arg. nâkâšâ, Har. nâxâša, Gur. See also nakasa, above.
misprint for fâsikâ.
misprint for naša.
transcription of Heb. nisân.

meaning: 'scatter, sprinkle, spread, strew about', 'passive', 'act. part. of nasnasa', 'pass. part. of nasnasa', 'who has a fly whisk', 'carries a fly whisk', 'fan, fly whisk, sprinkler'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. nâsnâ 'strew', Tna. nâsnâšâ, Amh. nâsnâšâ 'spread, scatter, wave (fly whisk), 'spread, sprinkle', Amh. 'fly whisk'. See also nsns II, and manasnâs 'fly whisk'.
perhaps from nsns I 'spread > spread out', the legs of the bed being separated.

meaning: 'spread stones for paving, pave with stones, hew stones, arrange in order, join closely', 'passive', 'pass. part. of nasaqa (see also məsuq)', 'joining closely' (n)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nasaqa 'put in proper order, line up', Akk. nasaqu (ši-stem) 'put in order, prepare'. On its connection with Heb. nešeq 'equipment, armory', see Rabin 1970:482. ETHIOPIC: Te. nâsəqa 'put things in order' (Reinisch 1887:285, who compares it with Bil. naseh).
from Amh. nâsr.

meaning: 'eagle, vulture, hawk', 'become like an eagle' (denominative of ʾansərt)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nasr, Soq. noyhir, Heb. nešer, Aram. nəsər, Syr. nešrâ, Md. nišra, Ug. nšr (Caquot 137), Akk. nasru. ETHIOPIC: Te. nasər, Tna. nasri, Amh. nasr; passed into Cushitic: Bil. niser.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. nasr qana 'kind of flute, bird with melodious voice'. Geez has also a form masr qânâ, and it is possible that masr qânâ is the original form meaning 'flute of Egypt' and that it became qânâ by folk etymology. I. Guidi, ZA 11 (1896), 410, n. 5 states “məsər (nasr) qânâ is una specie di tromba fatta a foggia di corno. Si chiama che perche somiglia ad uno segno musicale del Degg che occorre nel canto per la festa delle Nozzi Cana”. Praetorius, ZDMG 64 (1910), 622 states that G. nasr qânâ is an Amharic loanword.
for the meaning 'sway, move', see nsns I. The meaning 'decorate, adorn' can probably be derived from 'move, shake > glitter (through the move ment of shaking, flickering) > be decorated, adorned'.

meaning: 'judicial assembly which annuls or revokes a sentence on account of an appeal'; ('aqbəʾa nâstâ 'account for, give account'); is there any connection with nâstâ II?

origin_explanation: see also nâstâ I.

meaning: 'serpent-idol of bronze, contagious disease, plague, (T) field snake'
origin_explanation: transcription of Gk. neesthan, from Heb. nəḥuštân 'serpent-idol of bronze'.

SEMITIC: Heb. nissâ (piʿel of nsy) 'tempt, try', Aram. Syr. nassî (nsy), Syr. nasâyâ 'temptation', Md. nsâ 'tempt, try'. ETHIOPIC: G. nsw is undoubtedly reconstructed by K, and the actual form is mnsw; see mansawa, above.

probably borrowed from Ar. nasiya, as also in Te. nâsa. The root also occurs in the other Semitic languages: Sar. nsiy 'forget', Mh. nehû (h for š), Sh. nše, Heb. nâšâ (nšy), Syr. nšâ, Md. nš, Akk. našû, for which see Haupt, AJSL 22, p. 199.

meaning: 'pound, crush, hammer', 'be crushed, be pounded, be castrated'

'take, partake, receive, accept, capture, occupy, grasp, seize, catch, pick up, take up, raise, lift, set up, carry off, take away, fetch, take as wife'; (with gaṣṣ, in naṣa'a gaṣṣa 'have regard for, have respect for, show consideration'); (naṣa'a 'əda 'pay homage, greet by shaking hands'); naṣa'a 'take away, carry off'; 'anṣa'a 'take up, arouse, raise up, cause to rise, elevate, erect, set up, start, set out, stir up, set in motion, awaken, wake up (tr), resuscitate'; tanäṣa'a, passive of naṣa'a; tanṣa'a, see below; tanäṣa'a 'rise up, rise one against another'; 'astanäṣa'a (Lt) 'raise, bring up'; 'astanäṣa'a, caus. of tanäṣa'a; also 'reestablish, stir up, make rise, provoke, emulate, impel, arouse, elevate, lift up'; (astanäṣa'a mäkra 'decide'); naṣa'i, act. part. of naṣa'a; naṣu' (K*), pass. part. of naṣa'a; naṣa'at (N) 'taking, receiving, accepting, acceptance, seizure'; 'anṣa'i, participle of 'anṣa'a; tanṣa'ot 'rising up' (n); mänṣa'i, part. of 'anṣa'a; mänṣa'i 'gift (something that is accepted)'; mastanäṣa'i 'one who restores'; tanṣa'ot 'raising up' (n); tanṣa'e (Gr.213) 'resuscitated'; tanṣa'e 'ascent, rising, uprising, resurrection, Easter Sunday'; (bä'ala tänṣa'e 'Easter', lit. 'feast of resurrection');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naṣa'a 'rise, appear', Sar. nṣ' 'raise', Soq. misivoḥ 'pail' (Lit. 'something with which to carry'), Heb. nāsā' 'lift', Aram. ns' 'take, carry away', Ug. ns' 'lift, raise', Akk. našû 'lift, carry'. ETHIOPIC: Te. näṣa'a 'take, lift', Tna. näṣe'e, Amh. Arg. nässa, Har. näsa'a 'take, take away', Gur. nässa 'raise, lift'. For G. mänṣa'i 'gift', cp. Amh. männaša. Note that G. mänṣa'i is not to be compared with Heb. mas 'forced service' as suggested by Baumgartner 571 (where the Geez form is wrongly given as menšā'ā without the final >). The root passed into Cushitic: Be. nesā' 'get up, rise'.

meaning: 'bolt the door, fasten with bolts, close the door with bars, lock'; tanäṣägu, passive; naṣug 'bolted, closed with bars'; naṣig (M) 'bolting' (n); manṣäg, manṣäg (pl. manäṣäg, manäṣagt) 'bar, lock, (Lt) snare, shackle, fetter, (Sw.in D 640) inaccessible place';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the basic meaning is perhaps 'entangle' (>'bar, snare'), for which cp. Ar. naṣağa 'weave, plait' (Dillmann 639), Sar. h-nṣ-g 'fit together', Syr. naṣgā 'covered by clouds'. The connection with Heb. nṣg (hif'il) 'overtake' suggested by Bohl 525 and Koehler 637 is unlikely. Haupt, AJSL 26 (1909-10). 210 compares Ar. waṣağa 'intertwine, connect', for roots with initial w:n, see Noeldeke 1910:179ff. ETHIOPIC: Amh. šännägä 'bind tightly' (with metathesis). The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. masanäga 'bolt'. See also manṣägät.

meaning: 'destroy, demolish, abolish, ruin, overturn, extirpate, remove, pull down, tear down, tear asunder, overthrow, violate, frustrate, abrogate, annul, dismiss'; 'anṣaṭa (K*), causative; tanäṣaṭa, passive of naṣaṭa; naṣaṭi, act. part. of naṣaṭa; naṣuṭ, pass. part. of naṣaṭa; also 'dilapidated'; näṣṭat 'destruction, subversion, ruin, overthrow, abrogation'; manṣäṭi (L) 'one who destroys'; tanäṣṭot 'ruin(s)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naṣaṭa 'pull out, snatch away' (with metathesis, see Brockelmann 1908:273), Heb. nāṭaš 'put out' (Dillmann 634), Aram.Syr. naṭaš. Ludolf compares Heb. nāšat 'destroy'. ETHIOPIC: Te. näṣaṭa 'destroy', Tna. näṣaṭe 'be bed', Amh. näššäṭä; also mänšäšäṭä as if it were a denominative of *mnsṭ.

meaning: 'spread out, lay out, strew, scatter, cover with carpets, make flat'; 'anṣafa, causative; tanäṣafa, passive of naṣafa; naṣafi, act. part. of naṣafa; nsf 'strewn, spread out, (Lt) furnished (Gr.434) naduf 'bed'; naṣf 'cover, spread, (K) lining (of a garment)'; 'anṣafi (K*) 'who spreads'; manṣaf (pl. manäṣaf) 'bed, couch, blanket, rug, covering, spread, sack, sackcloth, mantle, (Lt) belt, (Lt) litter for carrying'; manṣafi (K*) 'who spreads, who strews'; manṣaf 'place where one spreads out something, (Lt) bed'; manṣaft (K*) 'ornament for the head, miter (see below, under 'Ethiopic');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naṣafa 'cover a young girl with a veil', Mh. he-ndauf 'spread', Soq. 'endef. ETHIOPIC: Te. näṣäfa 'spread (carpet, etc), make bed', Tna. 'anṣäfa, Amh. anäṭṭäfa, Har. anäṭäfa. For G. manṣaf, cp. also Chr.-Ar. manṣafa 'Humerale, das den Kopf verhüllt' considered by Graf 1 as coming from masnafa. If the metathesis suggested by Graf is correct, cp. also Heb. ṣānīp 'turban'. However, G. manṣaft could also be a metathesis of masnafa as suggested by K 650 and it would then be a transcription of Heb. miṣnepet 'turban'.
in the passage quoted by K 649, manṣaf can be rendered by 'bed, couch', and is then connected with nsf I.

M quotes T; however, T gives only 'mark, token' and not 'musical note'. The translation 'mark, token' given by T seems to be a free rendering of manṣaf in manṣafa dängəlännäha (Deut. 22:17), and can be translated 'the mantle of her virginity, the clothes of her virginity'. In this case manṣaf of nsf III is identical with manṣaf of nsf I.

meaning: 'be pure, be clean, be purified, be purged, be cleansed, be guiltless'; 'anṣäha 'purify, make clean, make clear, cleanse, make pure, keep pure, consider (hold) pure, purge, pronounce clean, hold innocent, hold guiltless'; tanäṣaha 'be purified, purify oneself, be cleansed'; tanäṣäha (K*) 'purify one another, (Gr) be purified (cleansed) little by little'; 'astanäṣaha 'purify, cleanse, clean, absolve (of a transgression), declare pure, expurgate, purge, separate'; naṣähi (K*), act. part. of naṣaha; naṣuh 'pure, clean, clear, spotless, absolved (of an oath), serene, cleansed, chaste, without blemish, innocent, blameless, guiltless, uncorrupted, sincere'; (bä-naṣuh, naṣuha, adverbial use of naṣuh); naṣh 'purity, pureness, purification, temperance, sincerity, chastity, innocence, integrity'; naṣihot 'purification'; näṣhat 'purity'; näṣhännä 'purity, chastity, innocence, integrity, (Gr.224) purification, sanctification'; 'anṣähi (K), act. part. of 'anṣäha; 'anṣähot (Lt) 'purification, cleansing'; manṣähi 'purifier, one who purifies'; manṣäh, mänṣäh (K*) 'means of purifying'; mastanäṣäh (K*) 'who purifies, who cleanses';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naṣaha 'be pure, sincere', Sar. nṣḥ-t 'friendship' (< 'sincerity, purity'), Soq. minṣaḥ 'threshing floor' (that is, 'place where the wheat is made pure'), Heb. neṣaḥ 'glory, eminence', Syr. nṣaḥ 'be brilliant, shine'. ETHIOPIC: Te. näṣäha 'be pure' (by dissimilation according to Brockelmann 1908:27), Tna. näṣhe, Amh. näṭṭäqa, Gur. näṭa. Tna. naṣuḥ 'white', Te. laṣuḥ, Gaf. näṣa, Har. näṣiḥ. Amh. naṣuḥ 'pure' (from Geez), näčč 'white', Gur. näčč passed into Cushitic: Sa. näṣah, Bil. lačäḥ.

meaning: 'detach, separate'; tanäṣala, passive; naṣul (K*) 'detached, separated'; näṣäl (K*) 'that which is detached, that which is separated';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naṣala 'fall off, be detached and fall'.
From Amh. nät; see also nat.
for nat'at, and see nat', below.

meaning: 'tent covering. (K•M) tanned hide dyed red'. natä'a (K•) 'spread a hide, dye hide red'; de-nominative.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. nat' 'leather mat used as table cloth or gaming board' (Dillmann 660). ETHIOPIC: Tna. nät'i 'tanned hide', Te. näta', nät 'mat of tanned hide'. See also nat'at, II above.

meaning: nata ኀተ 'flee, escape, withdraw, retreat, emigrate, gush, (Y) be split open, be divided in two'. ኀተ'a 'make flee, put to flight, rout'; ኀተ'i, act. part. of ኀተ'a; nat' (K•) 'flight'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. näta' 'flee' (Job 4:10) according to Praetorius 1890:25, note, compares G. nata with Ar. nt, a secondary form of int'a (nw). Brockelmann 1908:533 expresses doubts about this comparison. See also Greenspahn 139. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nät'e 'gush out', Har. nata'a 'em-pate' (in Leslau 1937:549). See also nt I.

meaning: 'tear to pieces, tear apart, lacerate, cut asunder, pluck out, spoil, deface, (Y) rob, (MA) open cotton bolls'. tanattafa, tanataffa, passive; natuf 'torn to pieces'; natäfe 'plucking, cutting, vexation (of spirit), spoiling, thoughtlessly dispersing'; natfat 'tearing to pieces, laceration, pulling asunder (n), vexation'; mänṭaf (T) 'fluffed cotton ready for carding' (one would expect an instrumental);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. natafa 'pluck out, pull out, tear out', Sh. nṭef 'pluck (hair)', Syr. nṭap 'pull, draw out'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nätfa 'cut off', Amh. nätṭäfä 'tear apart (cotton or the like)'; passed into Cushitic: nätäf. See also manṭafa.
See also mätfäy, above.

meaning: 'cease, stop, desist from, become less, fail, decrease, abate, omit, repeal, abrogate, remove, take off, take away, subtract'. ኀተገ, causative; also 'repeal, remove, omit, decrease (tr), abrogate, subtract, lessen'; tanatga 'be abated, decrease (intr), cease, di-minish'; nätug 'wanting, waning, imperfect, defective, removed'. nätg, see nätgä; nätgä, nätg, (Lt) nätg 'lack, defect, want, loss, interruption, blemish'; nätgat 'lack, defect'. nutäge 'defect, decrease, deficiency';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. nätga 'cease, become less, decrease', Amh. nätṭägä (K under nataga). related to nataka (see below).
related to nataga, above.

meaning: 'give'; tanatna (K•) 'be given'; ኀተነ 'give gifts'; nätna (T.M) 'offering to the church';
origin_explanation: probably transcription of Heb. näṭan 'give'.
see tantana II.
transcribes Gk. nathinim, from Heb. näṭunim 'those given to the temple, temple slaves'.

meaning: 'pull'. 'one would expect 'pull out' even though it is rendered by Amh. sabä 'pull, drag';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nataqa 'pull out', Heb. naṭaq 'pull off, drag away', Aram.-Syr. naṭaq. ETHIOPIC: the root is ntq in Tna. nätäqa 'pull out', Amh. nätäqa, Arg. nätṭäqa, Gur. nätäqa. The root ntq occurs in Amharic (Dillmann 162 under mšṭ: mäsäṭä zä nätäqä). See also nataqa, below.

meaning: 'be hot, be burning, be strong (beer, stew), be acid (beer, stew)'; nätur (K•) 'hot, burning';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. nätṭärä 'be excellent, be pure (beer, mead), be strong (beer, stew)'.

meaning: 'become disjointed'. Heb.Aram. nṣl (ḥif'il) 'tear away, deliver, (pi'el) 'strip off', Ph. nṣl (nif'al) 'escape', Syr. nṣal 'let drop'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. näṣla 'drop off, fall out, slip off', Tna. näṣälä 'separate into two, detach', Amh.Gur. nätṭälä 'detach, separate, make single'. The noun 'single-layered toga, sheet' is derived from the root nṣl: Te. näṣälu 'sheet', Tna. näṣälä 'single-layered toga', Amh.Gur. nätṭäla. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. naṭal.
Note also Tna. ʾamänsärä 'look across', denomina-tive of mänṣär.
