meaning: 'look, look at, look up to, look on, look toward, view, watch, regard, glance at, be on the watch, observe, consider, perceive, behold, examine, survey, turn the eyes to'; ʾanṣara (K) 'turn toward', look facing one, turn toward (pt), give a sign, make a sign, show'; (ʾanṣara fänoṭa 'turn toward'); (ʾanṣara gaṣṣo 'make for, direct oneself toward'); tanas(s)ara (K•) 'be seen, be visible, be looked at'; tanāṣara 'look at one another, find oneself face to face, look facing someone, be opposite one another, look at someone with animosity, be in opposition (that is, look in an unfriendly way), be likened'; ʾastanāṣara (K•), causative of tanāṣara; naṣṣāri, act. part. of naṣṣara; also 'spectator, inspector'; naṣṣur (K•) 'that is seen, that is evident'; naṣṣāre 'view, look, glance, gaze, viewing, sight, vision, aspect, appearance'; naṣṣarā, in ḥämāma naṣṣarā (Lt) 'evil eye'; (ba)naṣṣaro 'face to face'; naṣrot, naṣṣarot (Lt) 'aspect, sight'; ʾanṣär 'aspect, direction'; (ba-ʾanṣär 'facing each other, opposite'); ʾanṣär-a 'opposite, facing, across from, against, over against, toward, in front of'; (mangala ʾanṣära 'mangala ʾanṣära 'across'); ʾanṣäräwi 'who points at'; manṣar (K•T) 'spectacles'; manṣar 'the front part exposed to view, the opposite, against'; manṣar-a 'over against, in front of, opposite, across, toward';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naẓara 'regard, look, see', Sar. nẓr 'observe', Šh. nzor, Heb. näṣar 'watch, guard', Ph. nṣr, Aram.-Syr. naṭar, ancient Aram. nṣr, Ug. ngr, Md. ntr, Akk. naṣāru 'watch, observe'. On the root nṣr, nṭr in Semitic, see Wagner 83. ETHIOPIC: Te. näṣra 'look into the distance', Tna. näṣärä, Amh. nätṭärä 'observe', näṣärä (from Geez), Amh. anṣär 'opposite' (likewise from Geez). Note also Tna. ʾamänsärä 'look across', denomina-tive of mänṣär.
denominative of naṣorär, nadorär, for which see drr, ʾandorara.
to be identified with ṣry (see ṣaraya, below), prefix n and reduplication of r.

meaning: 'pull out, tear off, pate, strip, pluck out'; ʾanṣaya (K•), causative; tanṣaya, passive of naṣaya; also (Lt) 'fall'; tanāṣaya 'pinch one another, slander another'; ʾastanāṣaya (K•), causative of tanāṣaya; naṣuy, pass. part. of naṣaya; naṣyäy (K) 'that which is pulled out'; naṣyat (K•) 'pulling out' (n); naṣe (K•) 'that which is pulled out';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. naḍā (ndw) 'take off (a garment)', Sar. nḍw 'destroy, damage', Heb. nṣṣ (nṣy) 'fall in ruins', Akk. naṣū, neṣū 'tear out down, make an incision'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. näṣäyä, näṣnäṣä (reduplicative of nṣy reduced to nṣ) 'pull out', Te.Arg. näṣäa, Amh. näṣäa, Har. näṣäa, Gur. näṣäa; passed into Cushitic: Bil. laṣaḥ, Sa. näṣaḥ.
From Coptic nat 'fabric' (Dillmann 659).

phonetics: (K) 'furnaces'; probably transcribes Heb. tannurim (with metathe- sis), pl. of tannur 'furnace'.
origin_explanation: probably transcribes Heb. tannurim (with metathe- sis), pl. of tannur 'furnace'.

either directly from Gk. nautēs (> Latin nauta) or through Ar. nāwiyy; also Tna. notāwī. Noeldeke 1910:61 considers this root common to Arabic and Geez. In the other Semitic languages: Syr. nawtā, Judeo-Aram. nawwān, nawtay (with ṯ).
variant of nərə 'flee'.

phonetics: (K*) 'tear up, tear apart, make tear up. (Gr.433) carry off, carry away'.
meaning: 'tear up, tear apart, make tear up', 'carry off, carry away'

origin_explanation: `yəntəb` (K*), causative; `natb` 'drop, point, the four dots at the end of a sentence'; `natbat` 'drop'; `məntəb` 'liquid that falls drop by drop'; SEMITIC: related to nīf (nṭf): Ar. natafa 'fall in drops', Sar. m-nṭf-t 'altar for libations', Soq. nīf 'drop', Heb. nāṭap 'drop, drip', Aram.-Syr. naṭfā 'drop'. Md. nīf. For a Hebrew root nṭb 'drop, drip', figura- tively 'discourse', see Dahood 1966:240. ETHIOPIC: Te. nātba 'fall in drops', Tna. nātābā. Amh. nātābā. See also (ən)ṭabṭaba, and natāfa.

phonetics: (yəntəf) 'strain, filter, clarify, re- fine, purify, clean, (T) weed, polish, cover with metal to make it shiny, (MA) soak, bap- tize';
meaning: 'strain, filter, clarify, refine, purify, clean', 'weed', 'polish', 'cover with metal to make it shiny', 'soak', 'baptize'
origin_explanation: `yəntəf` (K*), causative; `tənafa`, passive of natāfa; `natuf` 'pure (gold), purified, refined, strained, cleansed, covered with metal'.

phonetics: (Gr.223), verbal noun of natāfa; məntəfī, (Lt) məntəf 'strainer, sieve, filter';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: nīf 'drop, drip', the straining and filtering being done by making drip, for which see natba above. There is also the possibility of comparing Ar. nazufa 'be clean, be tidy', causative 'clean, make tidy'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāṭfa 'filter beer', Amh. nāṭāfā, Amh. wāṭāfī 'sieve'. The root passed into Cushitic: Kam. naṭafo 'filter, strain', and Bil. wān- tābā 'sieve'.
from Amh. nāṭṭāqā; see also nataqa, above.

phonetics: (yəntər) 'scintil- late, sparkle, glare, shimmer, flash, be seen'; naṭr 'gleam, sparkle, splendor, glimmer, flash, lightning';
origin_explanation: (nəṭra mabraq 'flash of lightning'); SEMITIC: Ar. nadura 'be brilliant, be radiant'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāṭra 'sparkle, scintillate', Te. 'spurt out'.

phonetics: (yəntər) (T.K*.DTW) 'be clari- fied, be purified, be melted (butter, mineral)';
origin_explanation: `əntara` (K*.T.M.Y) 'clarify, purify, melt'; `naṭr` (K*) 'melted, purified, (T) gleam, flash'; `əntārī` (K*) 'metalworker, who melts (metal)'; ETHIOPIC: Amh. nāṭṭārā 'be melted, be purified', Arg. nāṭṭārā, Gur. nāṭārā. Amh. əntārāñña 'met- alworker' is to be derived from G. naṭr 'sparkle, splendor', as suggested by Praetorius 1879-89.
from Amh. nāṭṭārā 'jump'; also in Tna. nāṭārā.

instead of Dillmann 696 under naṭr 2 'ut vide- tur aurum et argentum'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāṭrī 'measure of weight', Amh. nāṭr, variant of lāṭər, from Ar. raṭl, unless it comes directly from Gk. litra, with a variant of l:n.
from Ar. naṭrūn, from Gk. nitron; also in Heb. neṭer. Aram. nitrā, Syr. neṭrā, Akk. nit(i)ru. For the Egyptian origin of this root, see Ellenbogen 197 and T. Lambdin, JAOS 73 (1953).152-3.

phonetics: (K*) 'jump with joy, prance, shout of joy';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. naṭṭa 'jump', Soq. neṭṭ 'shake'. Ug. nṭṭ and perhaps the Hebrew hapax tānūṭ (root nwt in Ps. 99:1) 'start, quake' (Caquot 166, n.b; see Greenspahn 135). ETHIOPIC: Amh. nāṭṭāṭā 'cause to gambol', anāṭā- nāṭā 'strut, prance'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. (ṭā)nāṭṭāṭā 'be scraped'.

phonetics: 'bother, nag, molest, nui- sance, wear out, tire out, importune, provoke, pressure, trouble, make afraid (Gr.433) be troublesome, be difficult to en- dure';
meaning: 'bother', 'nag', 'molest', 'nui- sance', 'wear out', 'tire out', 'importune', 'provoke', 'pressure', 'trouble', 'make afraid', 'be troublesome', 'be difficult to endure'
origin_explanation: (K), causative of the preceding meanings; `tənaṭaya` (Gr.460) 'be troubled, be bothered, be importuned, be annoyed'; `əntāyī`, `məntayī`, `məntay` (D.K*.DTW) 'trouble- some, annoying, irksome, vexatious, stub- born'; `məntayāwī` 'troublesome'; ETHIOPIC: perhaps Tna. nāṭāyā 'être réduit au dernier pion (dans un jeu), à la dernière ressource'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. nāwa; see *na.

phonetics: (KG), name of a city (Nini- veh), (T) 'storehouse of injustice';
meaning: 'name of a city (Niniveh)', 'storehouse of injustice', 'the epitome of a wicked city'

phonetics: (yənuh) 'be high, be tall, long, be lofty, be of long duration, be distant, be extended, be far off, be stretched out, re- pose, linger';
meaning: 'be high', 'be tall', 'long', 'be lofty', 'be of long duration', 'be distant', 'be extended', 'be far off', 'be stretched out', 'repose', 'linger'
origin_explanation: `ənōha` 'lengthen, prolong, let grow long, elevate, raise high, exalt, extend, protract (anger), defer, put forth (hand), remove to far off'; `ənōha manfasa` 'be forbearing, be long-suffer- ing)'; `tənawwəha` 'elevate oneself'.

`nuh` 'length, height, tallness, extent'; `(nuh-a` 'lengthwise'); `(nuha mawāʻəl` 'longevity'); `(nuha manfas` 'long-suffering, patience'); `(nuha maʻat` 'patience, forbearance'); `nuhat` 'length, patience'; `nohat` (Lt) 'length'; `nawwāh`, `nawwih` (fem. nawwāh) 'long, high, elevated, tall, lofty, extended, extensive, far away, far off, distant'; `nawwāh` 'a long time'; `(nawwāha` (nawwiha) manfas 'patient, forbear- ing)'; `nawwih-a` 'lengthwise, at a great distance'; `nawwāhāt` 'heights'; `ənwāhī` (Gr.460) 'who stretches, who pro- longs'; `ənəho manfas`, `ənəhota manfas` 'patience, for- bearance'; `məwāh` (K*) 'resting place'; SEMITIC: if the basic meaning is that of 'be stretched out, be extended, stretch out for a rest > repose, rest', the root is to be compared with Ar. nāha 'lie down upon his breast (camel)', Heb.Syr. nūh (nwh) 'be settled down, rest', Ug. nwh 'rest', Md. niāha. Akk. nāhu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāwhe, nōhe 'be long', Te. nāwwāha 'lengthen'.
either directly from Gk. naulon 'passage money' or through Ar. nawl 'freight'.

phonetics: (yənum) 'sleep, fall asleep, take a rest, die, (Lt) have intercourse';
origin_explanation: `nawwəma` 'have a deep sleep'; `ənoma`, `ənəma` 'make sleep, put to sleep'; `tənawwəma` 'slumber, lie down to sleep, (T.M) dream, be hidden'; `nawāmī` 'who sleeps'; `nawwām`, `nawwum` 'who sleeps, who slumbers'.

meaning: 'sleep' (n.), also euphemism for death; (za-nawām 'compline prayers'); nawāmē (Gr.222) 'sleep'; manawām 'sleeping room, dormitory';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nāma 'sleep', Heb.Aram.-Syr. nā'am (nwm.), Ug. nhmt 'sleep' (lit. 'sleep of death'; see Yasin 77, Caquot 508). Md. num 'sleep' (v.), Akk. nāmu 'slumber'. Note Akk. munattu (< munamtu) translated 'Morgenschlummer' (von Soden 672), but 'waking hours' (CAD, vol. 11, part 2, p. 200).

meaning: 'fresh fruits, first fruits, the month of Miyāziyā (April)';
origin_explanation: From Gk. neon, neuter of neos 'new, young'.

meaning: 'be tarnished, be infamous'; 'anwara 'stain, tarnish, blemish, reprimand, blame, give offense, disparage, disgrace'; tanawra (T) tanawara 'be tarnished, be depraved, be dishonored, be disparaged, be indecent'; nawr 'blemish, spot, stain, blot, disgrace, iniquity, (Gr.222) physical defect, infirmity'; (za'albo nawra 'immaculate'); (za'anbala nawr 'stainless'); nawwar 'nawwur 'faulty, defective, polluted, blameworthy, infamous'; manwərt (Gr) 'stain, spot';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nāwara 'abuse, insult'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nənwāra 'be dishonored, be disparaged', Amh. tā-nənwāra 'be shamed, be a disgrace', nawr 'disgrace, dishonor', Gur. nūwr 'disgrace'. passed into Cushitic: Sa. nawar, Kham. nūr.
denominative of nur, nār (see above).

meaning: 'vessel, utensil, instrument, implement, object, furniture, estate, property, possessions, goods, wealth, fortune, money, male genitals'; (nawāyā haql 'weapons'); (nawāyā gabr 'tools'); (nawāyā mazmur 'musical instruments'); (nawāyā wast, nawāyā karś 'bowels, intestines'); (nawāyā gome 'boat made of reeds lashed together', transcription of Heb. kalē gome'); (śərwa nawāy 'capital [money]');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: while the connection with Ar. 'inā' 'vessel' (Dillmann 674) is likely, the internal development presents difficulties. Brockelmann 1908:272 explains Ar. *'ināy (< *'inā') > *niyāy > nawāy by dissimilation. If the identification of G. nawāy with 'inā' can be proven satisfactorily, cp. also Mh. hinē 'utensil', Heb. 'ōni 'ship', Ug. any, Akk. unūtu 'vessel'. See also Fronzaroli 1972, vii, p. 642. For Indo-European 'ship' from 'vessel', see Buck 727. Note that the connection between G. nawāy and Ar. (Dat) fuwwārī 'dans des vases' (going back to 'anwara, nawāy), suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 72 (1918), 343, presents difficulties. ETHIOPIC: Tna. naway (also nawtī) 'possessions', nay, particle of possession; Te. nay 'belonging to', Amh. naway (from Geez). Rundgren 1955:213 derives Tna. nay from the element n to which he attributes a demonstrative, dative and genitive value. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. nuw 'utensil', Sa. nuwā.

meaning: 'be ready to be cooked'; nayā', ni' (K*) 'raw, uncooked'; nā'i (D), nā't (T) 'unleavened bread';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nā'a (ny') 'be raw', Śh. nu' 'uncooked', Heb. nā' 'half-cooked, raw (flesh)', Neo-Syr. nāyā 'raw' (perhaps from Arabic). ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. na'at 'unleavened bread', Amh. na'at (from Geez). See also (nā't), navata.
From Chr.-Ar. nā'ib, nāyib; also Te. nayab, title of the governor of Massawa.
perhaps from Ar. layyin 'tender, soft' (W. Müller).
from Persian nawrūz through Chr.-Ar. nayrūz.

SEMITIC: Ar. naza'a 'pull out', Heb. nāsa' 'pull out, set out > travel' (Dillmann 678, followed by Noel-Deke, ZDMG 40[1886].723). If Heb. nāsa' is to be compared, cp. also Ar. nasa'a 'travel', Aram. nasa' 'move', (hif'il) 'remove', Ug. ns' 'travel, remove', Ph. ns' 'tear away, uproot', Akk. nesū 'go away'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāz'a 'tear away, pull out', T. perhaps mānz'e 'pull with strength', a denominative from *mnz', from nz'. See also nazha.
perhaps to be connected with Ar. nasaba 'mention the relationship', nisba, name of the relationship to a father, mother, and so on, nsb becoming nzb by assimilation (W. Müller).

meaning: 'exhaust, empty, tear off, cut off, shake off, make fall (a leaf)'; tanazafa, passive;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. nazafa 'drain, exhaust', Soq. nezaf 'draw water'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāzfa 'fling away, push away'.

meaning: 'spread out (e.g. mat), spread grass or leaves on the floor'; tanazfa (K*), passive; related to nasafa, above.
'shawl-like garment of a single layer of cloth'; nazafa (K*) 'drape the nazifa'; tanazfa (K*) 'be draped';
origin_explanation: from Amh. nazifa 'white shawl-like garment', from Ar. nazifa 'clean'.
SEMITIC: Ar. nazaha 'keep away from, steer clear of'.

meaning: 'tear out, pull out, pluck off'; tanazha (Lt), passive; variant of naz'a, above.

'strew, sprinkle, spray, drench, dip'; 'anzaha, causative; tanazha, passive of nazha; nazāhi, act. part. of nazha; nazuh, pass. part. of nazha; nazhat, verbal noun of nazha; manzāh, manzāh, manzāh 'sprinkling, means of sprinkling';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. nadaha, nadaha 'sprinkle' (Dillmann 677), SAR. ndh 'sprinkle with impurity'; also Heb. nāzāh (nzy) 'sprinkle', Akk. nezzū. Brockelmann 1908:153 considers Ar. nadaha to be the original form, becoming nzh by assimilation. Barth 1893:52 compares Heb. nsh in nish-ām (Is.63:3,6) 'their bloodsports'. ETHIOPIC: Te. nāzha 'strew', Tna. nāzhe, Amh. nāzza; passed into Cushitic: Kham. nazeq.

meaning: 'go down, flow, spill over, (Y) seep (water), become damp (from seepage)';
origin_explanation: from Ar. nazala 'descend'; also in Te. nāzla 'go down'. The root also occurs in Heb. nzl 'trickle, flow' > 'descend'. Syr. nzl (pa'el) 'make go down'. See also manāzal.

meaning: 'vex, torment, cause pain, shake, agitate'; tanaznaza 'be vexed';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāznāzā 'shake', Te. nāznāzā 'jog', Amh. nāzənnāzā 'importune, pester', Gur.Amh. nənz 'drizzle for a long time', from the original meaning 'importune'.

meaning: 'bite, (Lt) tear to pieces, (T.Y) pierce, crunch, (MA) hit'; nazzara, same meaning; tanazzara, (K*) tanazzara, passive; nazāri (K*), act. part. of nazara; nazzur (M) 'torn to pieces, pierced, (Lt) pockmarked'; nazr, nazro (K*) 'piercing' (n); nazrat (M) 'biting (n), tearing to pieces (n)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. n-t-zr 'split' (intr.). Von Soden 772 compares G. nazara with Ar. nazara 'importune', Akk. nazāru 'blaspheme, curse'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. nāzārā 'tear to pieces, bite', Te. nāgra 'bite off', Amh. nāzzārā 'strike, hit', a-nāzzārā 'harass, pester', Gaf. tā-nāzzārā 'feel pain'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. a-nāzzārā 'squander'.

SEMITIC: Ar. nadara 'make a vow', SAr. ndr 'do penance', Soq. nider 'be consecrated', Heb. nzr (nif-'al) 'dedicate oneself', nādar 'make a vow', Aram.-Syr. nādar 'make a vow', nāzar 'abstain from, be continent', Ug. Md. ndr. ETHIOPIC: Amh. nāzzārā 'become a hermit' (from Geez). G. nāzrāwi 'hermit' is to be connected with the root nzr, while nāzrāwi 'Christian' meant origi-nally 'one from the city of Nazareth'. It may also be that nāzrāwi 'hermit' goes back to the meaning of nāzrāwi 'Christian' and has become restricted to that of 'hermit'.

Md. ndr. ETHIOPIC: Amh. nāzzārā 'become a hermit' (from Geez). G. nāzrāwi 'hermit' is to be connected with the root nzr, while nāzrāwi 'Christian' meant origi-nally 'one from the city of Nazareth'. It may also be that nāzrāwi 'hermit' goes back to the meaning of nāzrāwi 'Christian' and has become restricted to that of 'hermit'.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. nazāzā 'confess', Amh. (tä)nazzāzā 'console, comfort, hear confession (priest)', Gur. tä-nazzāzā.

meaning: 'comfort oneself, find comfort, con-sole oneself, find consolation, console one another, be comforted, be grateful'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. nazāzā 'confess', Amh. (tä)nazzāzā 'console, comfort, hear confession (priest)', Gur. tä-nazzāzā.

meaning: 'consolation, comfort, confession'; (mashafa nuzāze 'Book of Confession'); ('aba nuzāze 'father confessor')
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. nazāzā 'confess', Amh. (tä)nazzāzā 'console, comfort, hear confession (priest)', Gur. tä-nazzāzā.

meaning: 'come out of one's nose (food while one is asleep)'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. nāzzāzā, same as G. nazza.
